MCA Math scores show a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories, with 1,304 observations dropped due to missingness. Unlike the ACT Composite Score finding, the MCA Math gradient runs in the expected direction — higher scores are associated with better trajectory outcomes — though the pattern of significant contrasts is somewhat nuanced.

Consistently attached graduates show the highest mean MCA Math scores, significantly above intermittently disconnected (−0.14 points, p=0.000), broadly disconnected (−0.19 points, p=0.000), and persistently disconnected graduates (−0.20 points, p=0.000). The gaps between consistently attached and the three disconnected categories are all statistically robust, establishing consistent attachment as a clearly distinct higher-achieving group on math performance.

Among the three disconnection categories, the differences are smaller and less consistent. Persistently disconnected graduates score modestly below intermittently disconnected graduates (−0.06 points, p=0.040), but broadly and persistently disconnected graduates are statistically indistinguishable from each other (−0.01 points, p=0.980), as are broadly and intermittently disconnected graduates (−0.05 points, p=0.435). This creates a two-tier structure: consistently attached graduates at the top, and the three disconnection categories clustered together below, with only modest separation between them.

The MCA Math pattern contrasts meaningfully with the ACT Composite Score finding. Where higher ACT scores were paradoxically associated with persistent disconnection — likely reflecting out-of-state departure among higher-achieving graduates — MCA Math scores show a straightforward positive association with labor market attachment. This difference may reflect the broader population base of MCA Math, which includes all graduates rather than just ACT takers, diluting the out-of-state departure dynamic that dominated the ACT finding.

MCA Reading scores show a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories, with 1,475 observations dropped due to missingness. The pattern is notable for being simpler and less differentiated than MCA Math, with a single dominant contrast driving the overall association.

Consistently attached graduates show significantly higher mean MCA Reading scores than persistently disconnected graduates (−0.10 points, p=0.001) — the only statistically significant pairwise contrast in the entire analysis. All other comparisons are non-significant: consistently attached graduates are not distinguishable from intermittently disconnected (−0.05 points, p=0.438) or broadly disconnected graduates (−0.05 points, p=0.493), and the three disconnection categories are statistically indistinguishable from each other in every pairwise comparison.

This creates a notably different structure from MCA Math, where consistently attached graduates were significantly above all three disconnection categories. Here, MCA Reading separates only the two extremes — consistently attached and persistently disconnected — while the middle trajectory categories are indistinguishable from both ends. The magnitude of the key contrast is also smaller than in MCA Math (0.10 vs. 0.20 points), and the persistently disconnected versus intermittently disconnected contrast falls just short of significance (−0.06 points, p=0.054).

This is a somewhat surprising finding given that MCA Reading was the strongest academic achievement predictor in the point-in-time analysis and was retained as the primary achievement indicator in the full population multivariate models. In the trajectory context, MCA Math appears to carry more differentiation across disconnection categories than MCA Reading, suggesting that math achievement may be more directly relevant to the types of employment that produce consistent labor market attachment among non-college completers in this region — a finding worth noting when specifying multivariate models for the trajectory analysis.

MCA Science scores show no statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories (p-values ranging from 0.12 to 0.998 across all pairwise comparisons), with 4,026 observations dropped due to missingness — the largest missingness of any continuous variable in the trajectory analysis, reflecting the voluntary and self-selected nature of MCA Science participation noted throughout the analysis.

Every pairwise Tukey comparison is non-significant, and the absolute differences between disconnection categories are negligible — all contrasts fall within 0.06 scaled score points, which is effectively zero for practical purposes. Consistently attached graduates score modestly above the three disconnection categories, but none of these differences approach statistical significance. The three disconnection categories are entirely indistinguishable from each other.

This null finding contrasts with the point-in-time analysis, where MCA Science showed meaningful F- statistics and a clear gradient across workforce participation categories. The difference is likely attributable to two factors: the large missingness reducing statistical power, and the trajectory construction collapsing five years of outcomes into a single category that smooths over year-to-year variation where science achievement showed its clearest signal.

As in the full population analyses, MCA Science should be excluded from multivariate modeling of disconnection trajectories — both for its voluntary and self-selected nature and for its failure to show meaningful associations in the trajectory context. MCA Math is the stronger and more appropriate academic achievement measure for this analytical purpose.

Persistently disconnected-Intermittently disconnected 0.061144835 0.9982860
Persistently disconnected-Broadly disconnected        0.053581277 0.8633074

CTE achievement shows a statistically significant and meaningful association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.106), the strongest effect size among the high school accomplishments variables examined in the trajectory analysis and consistent with its growing importance identified in the point-in-time findings. The gradient across CTE categories is clear, monotonic, and substantively large — particularly at the extremes of the disconnection spectrum.

Persistent disconnection shows the most striking variation: graduates with no CTE engagement show a persistent disconnection rate of 63.3%, compared to 49.9% for CTE participants and 44.8% for CTE concentrators or completers. The gap between no CTE and CTE concentrators is more than 18 percentage points — the largest category-level difference observed for any variable in the trajectory analysis. Consistent attachment follows the mirror-image pattern: CTE concentrators show the highest consistent attachment rate (16.6%), followed by CTE participants (13.7%), and the no CTE group shows by far the lowest (8.6%) — less than half the rate of CTE concentrators.

Intermittent disconnection also shows a clear gradient (26.9%, 24.1%, 19.0% across CTE concentrators, participants, and no CTE respectively), reinforcing that stronger CTE engagement is associated with more partial rather than complete labor market engagement during transitional years — a profile consistent with eventual attachment. Broad disconnection shows modest variation across groups (11.7%, 12.3%, 9.2%) with no clear directional pattern.

The no CTE group deserves particular attention: nearly two-thirds experience persistent disconnection across their first five post-exit years, and fewer than one in eleven achieves consistent attachment. This is the most adverse trajectory profile of any subgroup defined by a high school accomplishment variable, and it reinforces the conclusion from the point-in-time analysis that the absence of both academic orientation and CTE engagement is the strongest single predictor of labor market detachment among non-college completers. CTE achievement is the variable most strongly and consistently associated with avoiding persistent disconnection in this population.

χ2=295.764 · df=6 · Cramer's V=0.106 · p=0.000

Total CTE courses taken shows a statistically significant and robust association with five-year disconnection trajectories, with every pairwise Tukey comparison statistically significant — the only continuous variable in the trajectory analysis to achieve full separation across all four disconnection categories. This result is consistent with its position as the strongest continuous predictor in the point-in-time analysis.

The gradient is clear, monotonic, and statistically unambiguous. Consistently attached graduates show the highest mean CTE course-taking, with every other group significantly below. Persistently disconnected graduates show the lowest mean, 1.52 courses below consistently attached graduates — by far the largest gap in the analysis. Intermittently disconnected graduates fall 0.35 courses below consistently attached, and broadly disconnected graduates fall 0.71 courses below, creating a clean four-tier ordering that maps directly onto the disconnection spectrum: more CTE courses, less disconnection.

Critically, every pairwise contrast between adjacent disconnection categories is also significant. Persistently disconnected graduates take 1.17 fewer courses than intermittently disconnected graduates (p=0.000), 0.81 fewer than broadly disconnected graduates (p=0.000), and broadly disconnected take 0.36 fewer than intermittently disconnected graduates (p=0.015). This complete separation across all categories distinguishes total CTE courses taken from every other continuous variable examined, including MCA Math and Reading, which only partially differentiated the disconnection spectrum.

Combined with the CTE achievement findings, the trajectory analysis confirms that CTE engagement — both in terms of depth of participation and level of achievement — is the most powerful high school accomplishment predictor of avoiding persistent labor market detachment among non-college completers. The dose-response character of the total CTE courses finding is particularly compelling: each additional CTE course is associated with movement toward better trajectory outcomes, with no threshold or ceiling effect visible in these data.

Persistently disconnected-Consistently attached       -1.5192638 -1.7785469
Broadly disconnected-Intermittently disconnected      -0.3589767 -0.6676209
Persistently disconnected-Intermittently disconnected -1.1670822 -1.3788536
Persistently disconnected-Broadly disconnected        -0.8081054 -1.0902697

Intermittently disconnected-Consistently attached     -0.06430616 0.0090819
Broadly disconnected-Consistently attached            -0.36816885 0.0000006
Persistently disconnected-Consistently attached       -1.25998072 0.0000000
Broadly disconnected-Intermittently disconnected      -0.05033260 0.0149142
Persistently disconnected-Intermittently disconnected -0.95531080 0.0000000
Persistently disconnected-Broadly disconnected        -0.52594122 0.0000000


Post-secondary

Summary

Postsecondary attendance shows a statistically significant and meaningful association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.132), the strongest effect size of any single variable examined in the trajectory analysis aside from CTE achievement. This is a substantively important finding: even among non-college completers, having attended postsecondary — without completing — is associated with meaningfully better trajectory outcomes than never having attended at all.

The differences across groups are substantial and consistent across the disconnection spectrum. Persistent disconnection is 12 percentage points lower among postsecondary attendees (42.8% vs. 54.9%), while consistent attachment is nearly seven percentage points higher (18.3% vs. 11.4%). Intermittent disconnection is also higher among postsecondary attendees (27.6% vs. 22.4%), suggesting that this group is more likely to experience partial rather than complete labor market engagement — a profile associated with eventual attachment rather than prolonged detachment. Broad disconnection is identical across groups (11.3% vs. 11.3%), indicating that the attended.ps advantage operates primarily at the extremes of the trajectory spectrum rather than across the middle categories.

The magnitude of these differences is notable given that this population — by definition — consists only of individuals who did not complete a credential. The postsecondary experience itself, even without credential attainment, appears to confer meaningful labor market trajectory advantages. Whether this reflects skills developed during postsecondary attendance, social networks and exposure to broader labor markets, or selection effects in which more motivated or able individuals both attend postsecondary and achieve better labor market outcomes cannot be determined from these data alone, but the association is robust and practically significant.

Among all variables examined in the trajectory analysis, postsecondary attendance ranks second only to CTE achievement in effect size, and its V=0.132 exceeds gender (V=0.109), special education status (V=0.075), ACT participation (V=0.075), and all high school characteristics variables. Its strong performance in the trajectory context — where the structural postsecondary attendance advantage visible in point-in-time analysis is removed — suggests that postsecondary attendance carries genuine predictive value for trajectory outcomes beyond simply delaying labor market entry.


attended.ps

         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)

χ2=228.779 · df=3 · Cramer's V=0.132 · p=0.000


Summary: Bivariate Analysis of Five-Year Disconnection Trajectories Among Non-College Completers

This analysis examined associations between 21 independent variables and five-year workforce disconnection trajectories among 13,177 Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who did not complete a postsecondary credential. Disconnection trajectories were constructed by classifying each individual as consistently attached (disconnected in 0 of 5 years), intermittently disconnected (1–2 years), broadly disconnected (3 years), or persistently disconnected (4–5 years) across the first five years after exiting formal education.

The overall distribution of disconnection trajectories is itself a primary finding: 50.4% of non-college completers are persistently disconnected, 24.3% intermittently disconnected, 11.3% broadly disconnected, and only 14.0% consistently attached. The modal experience among non- college completers in Southwest Minnesota during their first five post-exit years is near-complete labor market detachment — not temporary turbulence but sustained disconnection across most of the observation window.

Categorical Predictors Ranked by Effect Size (Cramer’s V)

1. CTE Achievement (V=0.106) is the strongest predictor of five-year disconnection trajectories among all variables examined. Its 18-percentage-point persistent disconnection gap between CTE concentrators (44.8%) and graduates with no CTE engagement (63.3%) is the largest category-level difference in the entire analysis. The no CTE group’s consistent attachment rate of 8.6% — less than half that of CTE concentrators — makes the absence of CTE engagement the single clearest marker of trajectory disadvantage among non-college completers.

2. Postsecondary Attendance (V=0.132) ranks second and is the strongest individual variable in the trajectory analysis when effect size alone is considered. Graduates who attended postsecondary without completing show 12 percentage points lower persistent disconnection (42.8% vs. 54.9%) and nearly seven points higher consistent attachment (18.3% vs. 11.4%) compared to those who never attended. Unlike the point-in-time analysis where the postsecondary attendance association was largely structural, the trajectory finding reflects a genuine labor market advantage associated with postsecondary exposure even without credential completion.

3. Gender (V=0.109) is the strongest demographic predictor, with females showing a persistent disconnection rate more than eight percentage points above males (55.4% vs. 46.8%) and a consistent attachment rate six points below (10.2% vs. 16.6%). The gender gap in trajectory outcomes is more pronounced than the point-in-time findings suggested, indicating that the cumulative burden of disconnection falls disproportionately on women in this population.

4. ACT Participation (V=0.075) is the strongest academic orientation predictor in the trajectory context, retaining meaningful predictive value after postsecondary attendance differences are absorbed into the population construction. ACT takers show seven points lower persistent disconnection and three points higher consistent attachment than non-takers.

5. Special Education Status (V=0.075) matches ACT participation in effect size and shows a persistent disconnection rate of 56.7% among special education graduates — eight points above non-special education graduates. The trajectory analysis reinforces the point-in-time finding that special education graduates face compounding labor market disadvantages not resolved within the first five years after high school.

6. English Learner Status (V=0.067) and Non-English Home Language (V=0.066) are effectively interchangeable in their trajectory findings, both showing persistent disconnection rates approaching 59% — among the highest of any demographic subgroup. Their near-identical effect sizes confirm that English learner status is the more precise of the two variables for multivariate modeling.

7. EDR (V=0.053) and Race/Ethnicity (V=0.053) share the same modest effect size. EDR shows a consistent geographic gradient with EDR 6E graduates experiencing the lowest persistent disconnection, while race/ethnicity contains the most extreme subgroup finding in the analysis: American Indian graduates show a 70.6% persistent disconnection rate — the highest of any subgroup defined by any variable examined.

8. Economic Status (V=0.047) is the weakest significant categorical predictor, with differences across groups small enough to question its independent contribution in multivariate models.

9. PSEO Participation (V=0.038) shows a negligible association after postsecondary attendance differences are absorbed, while AP Exam Participation (V=0.012, p=0.584) shows no significant association at all. Both can be excluded from multivariate modeling.

10. Community Demographic Description (V=0.025) produces a negligible association and can be confidently excluded from further analysis.

Continuous Predictors Ranked by Interpretive Clarity and Strength

1. Total CTE Courses Taken is the strongest and most clearly interpretable continuous predictor, producing the only fully significant Tukey comparison set in the trajectory analysis — every pairwise contrast across all four disconnection categories is statistically significant. The clean four-tier ordering, with consistently attached graduates showing 1.52 more courses than persistently disconnected graduates, has a dose-response quality that makes it the most compelling continuous predictor in the analysis.

2. MCA Math Score produces a two-tier structure in which consistently attached graduates score significantly above all three disconnection categories, with the disconnection groups clustered below. Its straightforward positive gradient — higher math achievement associated with better trajectory outcomes — makes it the preferred academic achievement measure for multivariate modeling over MCA Reading, which shows weaker differentiation in the trajectory context.

3. MCA Reading Score separates only the two extremes — consistently attached versus persistently disconnected — with all other pairwise comparisons non-significant. Despite its strength in point-in- time analysis, MCA Reading is a weaker differentiator of trajectory outcomes than MCA Math, suggesting math achievement is more directly relevant to the employment types that produce consistent labor market attachment in this region.

4. ACT Composite Score produces a counterintuitive finding: higher scores are associated with persistent disconnection rather than consistent attachment among ACT takers. This paradox is best interpreted as reflecting geographic departure — higher-achieving non-completers leaving Minnesota and thus appearing persistently disconnected in Minnesota employment records — rather than genuine labor market failure. This variable requires careful handling in multivariate models.

5. Average County Unemployment Rate shows a counterintuitive pattern in which persistently disconnected graduates come from lower-unemployment counties, consistent with the point-in-time interpretation that persistent disconnection is driven by absence from Minnesota records rather than local joblessness.

6. Average County Wages shows no significant association with trajectory outcomes and can be excluded from all further modeling.

7. MCA Science produces a null finding across all pairwise comparisons and should be excluded from multivariate models.

The Overarching Story

Three conclusions stand out from the full trajectory bivariate analysis.

First, persistent disconnection is the dominant experience among non-college completers, and its drivers are identifiable and concentrated in high school experiences rather than demographic characteristics. CTE engagement — both achievement level and course volume — is the most powerful available predictor of avoiding persistent disconnection, while the absence of any CTE engagement is the clearest single risk marker in the data.

Second, postsecondary attendance without completion confers genuine trajectory advantages beyond simply delaying labor market entry. The 12-percentage-point persistent disconnection gap between attendees and non-attendees, observable even after the structural postsecondary advantage is removed from the analysis, suggests that the postsecondary experience itself matters for labor market trajectory outcomes.

Third, demographic disadvantage — being female, a special education participant, an English learner, or American Indian — is associated with meaningfully higher persistent disconnection, but these effects are smaller than the CTE and postsecondary attendance effects. The implication is that interventions focused on high school experiences, particularly CTE engagement, are likely to have greater leverage on trajectory outcomes than demographic targeting alone — though the two are not mutually exclusive and the intersection of demographic disadvantage with low CTE engagement likely defines the highest-risk subpopulation in this analysis.


Relationship Between Individual Characteristics and the year-to-year refined disconnection categories

This analysis is focused on whether any variables that are most likely to show up in each of these categories. With such small numbers of individuals among the more refined workforce state categories, let’s combine some of them before the analysis. So let’s collapse into three primary categories;

  1. Group A: No MN employment record
  2. Group B: In-Region Marginal Employment
    1. This group will be only the Not meaningful_in_only and is our local underutilization group.
  3. Group C: Other Disconnected Employment
    1. This group contains all other Not meaningful employment categories.
      1. Not meaningful_out_only
      2. Not meaningful_unclassified_only
      3. Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_simultaneous
      4. Not meaningful_mixed_in_unclassified_simultaneous
      5. Not meaningful_mixed_out_unclassified_simultaneous
      6. Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_unclassified_simultaneous
      7. Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_sequential
      8. Not meaningful_mixed_in_unclassified_sequential
      9. Not meaningful_mixed_out_unclassified_sequential
      10. Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_unclassified_sequential

And just like before, we are going to measure the relationships between independent variables and the workforce participation categories for each after.ed.x. Here are the variables again;

  • Demographics
    • Gender
    • EconomicStatus
    • SpecialEdStatus
    • RaceEthnicity
    • english.learner
    • non.english.home
  • High school characteristics
    • Dem_Desc
    • edr
    • avg.unemp.rate
    • avg.wages.pct.state
  • High school academics
    • pseo.participant
    • took.ACT
    • ACTCompositeScore
    • ap.exam
    • MCA.M
    • MCA.R
    • MCA.S
    • cte.achievement
    • total.cte.courses.taken
  • Post-secondary
    • attended.ps

First we will collapse the workforce participation categories into the three groups.

new.groups <- master %>%
  select(PersonID, after.ed.1:after.ed.5) %>%
  pivot_longer(names_to = "after.ed", values_to = "wf.state", after.ed.1:after.ed.5) %>%
  mutate(new.wf.state = ifelse(wf.state %in% c("Non-sustained WF_out_only", "Non-sustained WF_unclassified_only", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_unclassified_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_out_unclassified_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_unclassified_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_sequential", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_unclassified_sequential", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_out_unclassified_sequential", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_unclassified_sequential"), "Non-sustained WF - other", wf.state),
         new.wf.state = fct_relevel(new.wf.state, "Sustained WF - Region", "Sustained WF - MN", "Non-sustained WF_in_only", "Non-sustained WF - other", "No MN employment record")) %>%
  select(PersonID, after.ed, new.wf.state) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = after.ed, values_from = new.wf.state)

data.analysis <- master %>%
  select(-after.ed.1:-after.ed.5) %>%
  left_join(new.groups, by = "PersonID")

kable(head(data.analysis))

Demographics

Here are the independent variables we are exploring;

  • Demographics

    • Gender

    • EconomicStatus

    • SpecialEdStatus

    • RaceEthnicity

    • english.learner

    • non.english.home

Summary

The annual analysis of detailed workforce participation states across the five post-exit years adds important texture to the aggregated trajectory findings, revealing not just how much demographic characteristics matter but how they shape the specific character of labor market disconnection over time. Several cross-cutting themes emerge from the full set of demographic variables.

The no MN employment record gap is the primary demographic fault line. Across virtually every demographic variable and every post-exit year, the most consistent and practically significant difference is in the no MN employment record category. This is true for gender (females increasingly absent), English learner status (persistently elevated absence), non- English home language (largest absolute gap of any variable at year 1), American Indian graduates (highest rates of any racial/ethnic group at every year), and Hispanic graduates (growing gap over time). The character of demographic disadvantage in this population is not primarily local underutilization — it is complete absence from formal Minnesota employment, a distinction that points toward out-of-state residence, informal employment, or structural labor market exclusion rather than insufficient local job availability.

Sustained statewide employment is a consistent secondary differentiator. Special education status, English learner status, non-English home language, and American Indian graduates all show substantially lower sustained statewide employment relative to their comparison groups, and these gaps generally widen over the five-year window. Reduced geographic labor market mobility — the inability to access employment outside the region — compounds the regional attachment disadvantage these groups experience and limits their overall employment outcomes even when regional employment rates are similar.

Each demographic group has a distinctive disconnection signature. Rather than producing uniform shifts across all workforce states, demographic characteristics tend to concentrate disadvantage in specific modes of disconnection. Special education graduates are primarily characterized by elevated in-region marginal employment and reduced statewide mobility — local underutilization. English learners show dual disadvantage spanning both MN record absence and in-region marginal employment. Non-English home graduates show the largest MN record absence gaps, particularly early. Black graduates show elevated other disconnected employment — fragmented multi-employer patterns — rather than MN record absence. American Indian graduates show near-complete MN record absence with very limited attachment of any kind. Gender differences manifest primarily in regional employment rates for males and MN record absence for females. Economic status shows the most nuanced and counterintuitive pattern, with disadvantaged graduates actually showing more local labor market engagement — albeit marginal — than non-disadvantaged graduates in early years.

Effect sizes are modest but consistent throughout. Cramer’s V values across the annual demographic analyses range from approximately 0.050 to 0.104, with most variables clustering between 0.065 and 0.090 across the five years. These are weak to modest associations by conventional standards, reinforcing the conclusion from the aggregated trajectory analysis that demographic characteristics, while meaningful in characterizing the type of disconnection, are secondary to high school accomplishment variables in explaining its overall prevalence.

Ranking the demographic variables by their overall contribution to the annual analysis, special education status and non-English home language show the highest and most consistent effect sizes in the annual data (peaking at V=0.104 and V=0.088 respectively), followed closely by English learner status (peak V=0.082) and gender (peak V=0.095). Race/ethnicity shows the most analytically rich subgroup variation despite its modest overall effect size (V=0.050–0.055), with American Indian and Black graduates displaying the most distinctive annual profiles. Economic status shows the weakest and most counterintuitive annual pattern (V=0.052–0.079), adding nuance but limited overall predictive clarity.

The annual demographic findings collectively reinforce three conclusions for multivariate modeling: first, that the no MN employment record category warrants particular analytical attention as the primary site of demographic disadvantage; second, that special education status and English learner status are the demographic variables most likely to contribute independent explanatory value beyond other correlated predictors; and third, that the distinctive profiles of specific racial and ethnic subgroups — particularly American Indian graduates — may require subgroup- specific analyses that aggregate Cramer’s V values cannot adequately capture.


Gender

Gender shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V increasing from year 1 (V=0.064) through years 3 and 5 (V=0.091) and peaking at year 4 (V=0.095). The growing association over time reflects the same widening gender gap identified in both the point-in-time and trajectory analyses, now visible on an annual basis.

The dominant and most consistent gender difference across all five years is in sustained regional employment, where males substantially outpace females at every time point. The gap starts at five percentage points in year 1 (24.3% vs. 19.3%), widens to seven points by year 2 (27.7% vs. 20.8%), and stabilizes around seven percentage points in years 3 through 5 (29.0% vs. 21.8%, 29.4% vs. 22.2%, 29.4% vs. 22.3%). This persistent and growing regional employment advantage for males is the clearest and most durable gender signal in the annual data.

The no MN employment record category shows the second most consistent gender difference, with females exceeding males at every time point. The gap starts modestly at year 1 (30.1% vs. 27.8%) and grows progressively — reaching 33.6% vs. 30.8% at year 3, 36.2% vs. 31.4% at year 4, and 37.8% vs. 32.8% at year 5. By year 5, nearly 38% of females carry no MN employment record compared to 33% of males — a five- percentage-point gap representing a meaningful and growing divergence in Minnesota labor market presence.

The non-sustained workforce participation categories tell a complementary story. In year 1, females show higher rates of both in-region marginal employment (20.9% vs. 18.9%) and other disconnected employment (20.2% vs. 18.8%) than males. By years 3 through 5, these gaps narrow or reverse slightly as the no MN employment record category absorbs a growing share of females. This suggests that some females who were marginally attached to the regional labor market in early years transition toward complete absence from Minnesota employment records rather than toward sustained employment — a trajectory consistent with the persistent disconnection findings in the aggregated analysis.

Sustained statewide employment shows minimal gender differences at year 1 (9.4% vs. 10.2%) that remain small but slightly favor males through year 5 (14.7% vs. 16.2%), consistent with males showing modestly greater out-of-region labor market mobility alongside their stronger regional employment rates.

Overall, the annual gender findings reinforce and enrich the trajectory analysis: males consistently achieve higher sustained regional employment while females are increasingly concentrated in the no MN employment record category over time. The character of female disconnection is not primarily local underutilization — females are not disproportionately in in-region marginal employment — but rather complete absence from formal Minnesota employment, a distinction with important implications for how gender-based labor market disadvantage is understood and addressed in this population.

economic.status

Economic status shows a statistically significant but weak association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V remaining consistently modest and showing modest growth over time (V=0.052 at year 1, rising to V=0.079 at year 5). The overall pattern is characterized by small but consistent differences that reveal an interesting and somewhat counterintuitive story about how economic disadvantage shapes the character of disconnection rather than its overall prevalence.

At year 1, the most notable difference is not in sustained employment or no MN employment record but in the non-sustained workforce participation categories. Economically disadvantaged graduates show higher rates of both in-region marginal employment (20.7% vs. 18.7%) and other disconnected employment (20.2% vs. 18.5%), while non-disadvantaged graduates show a higher no MN employment record rate (30.3% vs. 27.2%). This pattern — disadvantaged graduates more likely to be working locally but not sustainably, non-disadvantaged graduates more likely to be absent from Minnesota records — runs counter to the simple narrative that economic disadvantage produces worse labor market outcomes across the board. Sustained regional employment is nearly identical across groups at year 1 (21.7% vs. 22.8%), while non- disadvantaged graduates show modestly higher sustained statewide employment (10.8% vs. 9.0%).

By years 2 and 3, the pattern shifts. The no MN employment record gap narrows and then reverses, with non-disadvantaged graduates showing higher rates in years 2 and 3 (32.1% vs. 29.1% at year 2, 33.2% vs. 30.8% at year 3). Disadvantaged graduates continue to show higher in-region marginal employment (17.8% vs. 14.3% at year 2, 15.2% vs. 11.8% at year 3), suggesting they remain more engaged in local labor markets — however tenuously — while non-disadvantaged graduates are more likely to drift from Minnesota employment records during these middle years.

At years 4 and 5, the statewide employment gap solidifies as the most consistent and interpretable difference. Non-disadvantaged graduates show meaningfully higher sustained statewide employment (15.7% vs. 13.1% at year 4, 17.3% vs. 14.0% at year 5), while sustained regional employment converges nearly completely (26.9% vs. 25.9% at year 4, 26.8% vs. 26.2% at year 5). Disadvantaged graduates continue to show higher in-region marginal employment at years 4 and 5 (13.7% vs. 10.5%, 12.3% vs. 9.2%), while no MN employment record rates converge toward similarity by year 5 (35.9% vs. 33.8%).

The annual economic status findings add important nuance to the trajectory analysis. Economic disadvantage does not primarily manifest as greater absence from Minnesota employment — if anything, disadvantaged graduates show greater local labor market engagement, albeit in marginal rather than sustained forms. The more durable economic status disadvantage is in sustained statewide employment, where non-disadvantaged graduates maintain a consistent three-percentage-point advantage across years 3 through 5. The character of disadvantaged graduates’ disconnection is local underutilization rather than complete labor market absence — a distinction that has different implications for intervention than the patterns observed for gender or English learner status.

SpecialEdStatus

Special education status shows a statistically significant and consistent association with detailed annual workforce participation states across all five post-exit years, with Cramer’s V strongest at years 1 and 5 (V=0.103, 0.104) and modestly lower in the middle years (V=0.088–0.095). The pattern is distinctive and differs meaningfully from both gender and economic status in its character: the primary signature of special education status is not absence from Minnesota employment records but rather a persistent trade- off between in-region marginal employment and statewide sustained employment.

The most striking and consistent finding across all five years is in sustained statewide employment, where the gap between non-special education and special education graduates is large and grows over time. At year 1, non- special education graduates show a sustained statewide employment rate of 11.1% compared to only 5.9% among special education graduates — a gap of more than five percentage points that widens to 7.3 points at year 2 (12.7% vs. 7.3%), 5.5 points at year 3 (14.3% vs. 8.8%), 6.2 points at year 4 (15.9% vs. 9.7%), and 7.1 points at year 5 (17.3% vs. 10.2%). This persistent and growing statewide employment deficit is the most durable annual finding for special education status and is consistent with the point-in-time and trajectory findings showing reduced labor market mobility outside the region among this group.

The in-region marginal employment category tells the complementary story. At year 1, special education graduates show substantially higher in-region marginal employment (24.9% vs. 18.1%) — a nearly seven-percentage- point gap indicating that special education graduates are disproportionately working locally but below sustained thresholds. This gap narrows progressively over the five years (20.3% vs. 14.8% at year 2, 17.4% vs. 12.4% at year 3, 15.5% vs. 11.1% at year 4, 14.7% vs. 9.6% at year 5) but never closes, remaining at five percentage points by year 5. The persistent elevation of in-region marginal employment among special education graduates across all five years indicates chronic local underutilization rather than a transitional early-career pattern.

Sustained regional employment shows modest differences early — non-special education graduates lead by three points at year 1 (23.0% vs. 20.0%) and two points at year 2 (25.4% vs. 23.1%) — but converges nearly completely by years 3 through 5 (26.1% vs. 25.6%, 26.5% vs. 26.2%, 26.4% vs. 26.6%). By the later post- exit years, special education and non-special education graduates achieve sustained regional employment at virtually identical rates, suggesting that those special education graduates who do achieve sustained attachment do so regionally rather than statewide.

No MN employment record rates show the smallest and least consistent differences across groups, ranging from special education graduates showing slightly higher rates at years 1 and 2 (30.6% vs. 28.2%, 32.4% vs. 30.0%) to essentially identical rates at years 3 through 5 (32.5% vs. 31.8%, 34.1% vs. 33.1%, 35.1% vs. 34.8%). This convergence distinguishes special education status from gender and English learner status, where no MN employment record gaps persist or grow over time.

The annual special education findings tell a coherent and distinctive story: special education graduates are not more likely to be absent from Minnesota employment records, but they are substantially more likely to be stuck in in-region marginal employment and substantially less likely to achieve sustained statewide employment. Their disconnection is characterized by local underutilization and limited geographic mobility rather than complete labor market absence — a profile that differs meaningfully from other disadvantaged groups and has specific implications for the types of workforce interventions most likely to be effective for this population.

RaceEthnicity

Race/ethnicity shows a statistically significant but weak association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V remaining remarkably stable and small across all five years (V=0.050, 0.054, 0.053, 0.055, 0.055). Despite the consistently modest overall effect size, the annual data reveal substantively meaningful and distinctive patterns for specific racial and ethnic groups that the aggregate Cramer’s V obscures.

American Indian graduates show the most consistently adverse annual profile of any racial or ethnic group. No MN employment record is by far their most common annual state at every time point, ranging from 43.4% at year 1 to a peak of 46.4% at year 4 before settling at 44.7% at year 5 — consistently 15 to 16 percentage points above White graduates and the highest no MN record rate of any group at every year. Sustained regional employment starts at a very low 8.1% at year 1 and grows modestly to 18.7% by year 5, suggesting some late-developing regional attachment but from a very low starting point. In-region marginal employment is elevated in the early years (26.0% at year 1, 26.8% at year 2) before declining, indicating some local labor market engagement that does not convert to sustained regional employment at the same rate as other groups. Sustained statewide employment is persistently the lowest of any group at every time point (6.8%, 6.4%, 8.5%, 6.8%, 6.8%), well below the overall rate and declining in relative terms over the five years.

Asian/Pacific Islander graduates show a distinctive profile defined by high no MN employment record rates combined with notably high sustained statewide employment. No MN record ranges from 37.1% to 42.2% across the five years — consistently above White graduates — while sustained statewide employment is the highest of any group at years 1, 3, 4, and 5 (12.9%, 17.3%, 16.5%, 16.3%), suggesting that Asian/Pacific Islander graduates who do achieve sustained employment disproportionately do so outside the region. Sustained regional employment is consistently below the overall rate (18.0%, 18.8%, 18.6%, 18.4%, 20.4%), and in-region marginal employment is low relative to other groups, particularly in later years (8.2% at year 5). The profile suggests geographic bifurcation — either absent from Minnesota records or employed statewide — with limited regional attachment.

Black graduates show the most unusual and consistent annual pattern of any group: persistently elevated other disconnected employment relative to all other groups at every time point (26.4%, 30.0%, 29.6%, 26.4%, 26.1%). This category — which includes out-of-region non-sustained work and various mixed employment patterns — is nearly double the overall rate at every year, while in-region marginal employment is relatively low. This suggests that Black graduates are more likely than other groups to have fragmented multi-employer or out-of-region employment experiences rather than the complete Minnesota labor market absence seen in American Indian and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates or the local underutilization seen in other groups. No MN record rates for Black graduates are consistently the lowest of any non-White group and closest to White graduates (25.4%, 29.6%, 29.6%, 31.3%, 37.5%), though they grow substantially over five years. Sustained regional employment grows from 20.2% to 17.9% — relatively flat — while statewide employment ranges from 7.5% to 11.1%.

Hispanic graduates show a profile closer to White graduates than to other minority groups on most dimensions. Sustained regional employment grows from 22.6% at year 1 to 25.4% at year 5, near the overall rate. No MN employment record is elevated relative to White graduates at every year (33.8%, 34.5%, 36.4%, 38.2%, 38.9%) and grows more steeply over time, suggesting increasing geographic departure or labor market withdrawal in later post-exit years. Sustained statewide employment is consistently below the overall rate (7.1%, 7.6%, 9.4%, 9.9%, 10.7%), and in-region marginal employment is near the overall rate throughout. The Hispanic profile is characterized by modest regional attachment similar to White graduates but with a growing no MN record gap that widens over the five-year window.

White graduates show the most favorable profile overall, with sustained regional employment growing steadily from 22.8% to 27.3% and sustained statewide employment growing from 10.2% to 16.6% — both the highest rates of any group by year 5. No MN employment record grows from 27.4% to 33.7%, the lowest of any group throughout the entire five-year window. In- region marginal employment declines from 19.9% to 10.7%, consistent with conversion toward sustained regional employment over time.

Overall, the annual race/ethnicity findings reveal four distinct labor market profiles among non-college completers: American Indian graduates characterized by persistent MN record absence and limited labor market attachment of any kind; Asian/Pacific Islander graduates characterized by geographic bifurcation between MN absence and statewide employment; Black graduates characterized by fragmented multi-employer or out-of-region employment rather than complete absence; and Hispanic and White graduates showing broadly similar patterns of regional attachment with Hispanic graduates showing a growing no MN record gap over time. These group-specific patterns are analytically important and would be obscured by the modest overall Cramer’s V values alone.

  • after.ed.1
  • after.ed.2
  • after.ed.3
  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5

χ2=161.371 · df=16 · Cramer's V=0.055 · p=0.000


english.learner

English learner status shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest but relatively stable across the five years (V=0.070, 0.071, 0.067, 0.080, 0.082), with a slight strengthening in the later years. The pattern is consistent and interpretable, defined primarily by two persistent gaps: elevated no MN employment record rates and substantially reduced sustained statewide employment among English learners relative to non-English learners.

The no MN employment record gap is the most prominent and consistent finding across all five years. English learners show substantially higher no MN record rates at every time point: 36.0% vs. 27.7% at year 1, 35.9% vs. 29.8% at year 2, 37.0% vs. 31.2% at year 3, 38.1% vs. 32.7% at year 4, and 39.8% vs. 34.1% at year 5. The gap holds at approximately six to eight percentage points throughout and grows slightly over time — from 8.3 points at year 1 to 5.7 points at year 5, with a widening at year 4 and 5. This persistent and growing absence from Minnesota employment records is the defining characteristic of English learner labor market experience in this population and mirrors the pattern identified across all previous analyses.

Sustained statewide employment shows the second most consistent and substantial difference. English learners show notably lower statewide employment at every time point: 7.0% vs. 10.3% at year 1, 6.9% vs. 12.1% at year 2, 9.2% vs. 13.6% at year 3, 9.7% vs. 15.1% at year 4, and 10.6% vs. 16.4% at year 5. The gap widens from 3.3 points at year 1 to 5.8 points by year 5, indicating that English learners are increasingly disadvantaged in accessing sustained employment outside the region as careers develop — a pattern consistent with reduced geographic labor market mobility.

In-region marginal employment shows an interesting and somewhat counterintuitive pattern. English learners show higher rates of in-region marginal employment than non-English learners at years 1 through 4 (19.7% vs. 19.7% at year 1 — identical — then 18.7% vs. 15.7%, 16.6% vs. 13.1%, 16.6% vs. 11.5%), before converging somewhat at year 5 (15.1% vs. 10.2%). This elevated in-region marginal employment, combined with lower no MN record rates than might be expected given the overall pattern, suggests that English learners are more likely than non-English learners to be working locally in fragmented or below-threshold employment rather than being entirely absent from Minnesota — particularly in the early post-exit years.

Sustained regional employment shows modest but consistent differences favoring non-English learners across all five years (22.4% vs. 21.3% at year 1, growing to 27.0% vs. 23.1% at year 5), with the gap widening over time. Other disconnected employment shows similar rates across groups at most time points, with English learners slightly below non-English learners in most years.

The annual English learner findings tell a coherent story of dual disadvantage: English learners are both more likely to be absent from Minnesota employment records and more likely to be trapped in in-region marginal employment when they are present in the labor market. Their disconnection is not concentrated in a single mode but spans both the complete absence end and the local underutilization end of the spectrum — a pattern that distinguishes them from other demographic groups and may reflect the particular challenges of limited English proficiency in accessing stable employment in both regional and statewide labor markets.

  • after.ed.5

χ2=88.270 · df=4 · Cramer's V=0.082 · p=0.000


non.english.home

Non-English home language shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest and relatively stable across the five years (V=0.088, 0.078, 0.077, 0.076, 0.075), declining slightly over time in contrast to the slight growth observed for English learner status. The overall pattern closely mirrors English learner status in direction and character, consistent with the substantial overlap between these two variables identified throughout the analysis.

The no MN employment record gap is again the dominant and most consistent finding. Graduates from non-English speaking homes show substantially higher no MN record rates at every time point: 39.0% vs. 27.3% at year 1 — a gap of 11.7 percentage points, notably larger than the 8.3-point English learner gap at year 1. This gap remains large and grows over time: 39.6% vs. 29.2% at year 2 (10.4 points), 41.1% vs. 30.6% at year 3 (10.5 points), 42.0% vs. 32.1% at year 4 (9.9 points), and 43.4% vs. 33.6% at year 5 (9.8 points). The no MN employment record rate for non-English home graduates reaches 43.4% by year 5 — higher than the 39.8% observed for English learners at the same time point — suggesting that non-English home language captures a somewhat broader group with even more pronounced Minnesota labor market absence.

Sustained statewide employment shows a consistent and growing deficit for non-English home graduates across all five years: 8.6% vs. 10.1% at year 1, 8.2% vs. 11.9% at year 2, 10.5% vs. 13.4% at year 3, 10.4% vs. 15.0% at year 4, and 11.4% vs. 16.2% at year 5. The gap widens from 1.5 points at year 1 to 4.8 points at year 5, a trajectory of growing geographic disadvantage in accessing out-of-region sustained employment that parallels the English learner finding.

Sustained regional employment shows a modest but consistent gap favoring English home graduates across all five years, ranging from 1.7 points at year 1 (22.5% vs. 20.8%) widening to 4.5 points at year 5 (27.0% vs. 22.5%).

In-region marginal employment and other disconnected employment show smaller and less consistent differences across groups. Notably, non-English home graduates show lower in-region marginal employment than English home graduates at years 1 and 2 (16.0% vs. 20.2%, 15.3% vs. 16.2%), converging to near-identical rates by years 3 through 5. This contrasts with the English learner finding where elevated in-region marginal employment was a more consistent feature, suggesting that non-English home language captures a somewhat different within-group dynamic, with less local labor market engagement and more complete Minnesota absence.

The most analytically important distinction between non-English home language and English learner status in the annual data is in the no MN employment record gap at year 1: 11.7 points for non-English home versus 8.3 points for English learner status. This larger initial gap, combined with the declining Cramer’s V for non-English home language over time, suggests that non-English home language is a somewhat stronger predictor of early Minnesota labor market absence but may be capturing a different — and potentially broader — mechanism than English learner status alone. As noted throughout the analysis, the independent contributions of these two overlapping variables will require multivariate modeling to disentangle.

  • after.ed.1
  • after.ed.2
  • after.ed.3
  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5

χ2=73.486 · df=4 · Cramer's V=0.075 · p=0.000



High School Characteristics

Here are the variables we are going to explore;

  • Dem_Desc
  • edr
  • avg.unemp.rate
  • avg.wages.pct.state

Summary

The annual analysis of detailed workforce participation states reveals that high school characteristics variables are more informative in this framework than the aggregated trajectory analysis suggested, with both EDR and community demographic description showing meaningfully larger effect sizes when examined against the five detailed annual states than against the four aggregated trajectory categories.

Economic Development Region is the strongest variable in this group across all five post-exit years, with Cramer’s V growing consistently from V=0.110 at year 1 to a peak of V=0.121 at year 4 before settling at V=0.115 at year 5. Its defining feature is the sustained statewide employment gap between EDR 6E and the other two EDRs — a gap that grows from 5 percentage points at year 1 to nearly 10 points by year 5. EDR 6E graduates are consistently more likely to achieve sustained statewide employment and less likely to carry no MN employment record, while EDR 8 graduates show the reverse — higher regional attachment among those who find sustained work but substantially greater MN record absence overall. Sustained regional employment converges across EDRs by years 2 through 5, confirming that the regional economy absorbs similar shares of non-completers regardless of sub-region. The other disconnected employment category is notably elevated for EDR 6E throughout, reflecting greater exposure to diverse and fragmented labor market options in the Southwest Central region. EDR is a strong candidate for retention in multivariate models of detailed annual workforce states.

Community Demographic Description shows a notably stronger annual association (V=0.073 to 0.087) than its aggregated trajectory association (V=0.025) suggested, and warrants reconsideration as a result. Its most distinctive and durable feature is the sustained statewide employment advantage of urban/town/rural mix graduates, which grows from 13.8% at year 1 to 22.7% by year 5 — more than double the entirely rural rate at year 5 and consistently the largest category-level difference for this variable. Town/rural mix graduates maintain the highest sustained regional employment at every year. Entirely rural graduates show the most concerning trajectory, with no MN employment record rates growing from 35.3% at year 1 to 42.3% by year 5 — the highest of any community type and growing substantially. The annual findings suggest Dem_Desc adds geographic labor market context beyond what EDR captures alone, though its independent contribution in multivariate models alongside EDR remains to be assessed.

Average County Unemployment Rate produces the strongest and most interpretable continuous variable findings in this group, with F-statistics growing from 43.0 at year 1 to 47.9 at year 5. Its signature finding — that sustained statewide employment is consistently associated with the highest county unemployment environments of any workforce state — is present from the very first post-exit year and grows more pronounced over time, providing the clearest evidence of a labor market push dynamic in the entire high school characteristics group. A secondary and analytically important finding is the trajectory of the no MN employment record group, which starts indistinguishable from sustained regional workers on county unemployment but gradually separates toward lower-unemployment environments by years 4 and 5, suggesting that by later post-exit years the MN-record-absent population increasingly reflects voluntary out-of-state departure from favorable local conditions rather than displacement.

Average County Wages as Percent of State Average produces statistically significant associations at every year but with negligible absolute differences — all contrasts within 0.02 percentage points of the state average. Its only durable and practically interpretable finding mirrors the unemployment rate variable: sustained statewide workers consistently originate from lower-wage counties, adding modest confirmatory evidence for the push interpretation. The inconsistent pattern for the no MN employment record group and the negligible magnitude of all differences support excluding this variable from multivariate models in favor of county unemployment rate.

Two cross-cutting observations emerge from the full high school characteristics group. First, the annual detailed analysis consistently reveals stronger and more interpretable associations than the aggregated trajectory framework, particularly for EDR and community demographic description — a finding that justifies the additional analytical effort of the annual approach. Second, the labor market push dynamic — whereby less favorable local conditions (higher unemployment, lower wages) are associated with out-of-region employment — is the most coherent and consistent theme across all four variables, operating through both geographic sub-region and county-level labor market conditions to shape where non-college completers end up in the labor market rather than whether they find work at all.


Dem_Desc

Community demographic description shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V growing modestly over time (V=0.073 at year 1, peaking at V=0.087 at year 4 before settling at V=0.082 at year 5). This represents a notable reversal from the aggregated trajectory analysis, where Dem_Desc produced a negligible V=0.025. The annual detailed analysis reveals that the variable’s association with workforce participation is real and meaningful — it was simply obscured by the aggregated four-category trajectory framework.

The most distinctive and consistent finding across all five years is the behavior of the urban/town/rural mix category on sustained statewide employment. Urban/town/ rural mix graduates show substantially higher statewide employment than either entirely rural or town/rural mix graduates at every time point: 13.8% vs. 8.2% and 9.4% at year 1, 16.7% vs. 9.2% and 10.8% at year 2, 19.1% vs. 10.4% and 12.3% at year 3, 21.4% vs. 11.6% and 13.6% at year 4, and 22.7% vs. 12.2% and 14.9% at year 5. The gap between urban/town/rural mix and the other two community types grows steadily over the five years and reaches more than ten percentage points by year 5, making this the largest and most durable community type difference in the annual data. This pattern is consistent with the point-in-time analysis finding that urban/town/ rural mix graduates show growing statewide labor market mobility over time.

The mirror image of this statewide employment advantage is a sustained regional employment deficit for urban/ town/rural mix graduates. They show the lowest sustained regional employment of any community type at every year: 17.1% vs. 20.3% and 23.6% at year 1, declining slightly relative to other groups but remaining consistently the lowest through year 5 (19.4% vs. 25.5% and 27.9%). The town/rural mix maintains the highest sustained regional employment across all five years, consistent with its role as the most regionally anchored community type throughout the analysis.

Entirely rural graduates show the most distinctive no MN employment record profile. Their no MN record rates are the highest of any community type at every time point — 35.3% at year 1, 37.2% at year 2, 37.5% at year 3, 40.1% at year 4, and 42.3% at year 5 — growing substantially and consistently over the five years. By year 5, more than 42% of entirely rural graduates carry no MN employment record, more than eight percentage points above town/rural mix graduates (33.4%) and six points above urban/town/rural mix graduates (36.0%). This growing and large no MN record gap distinguishes entirely rural graduates as the community type most at risk of complete Minnesota labor market absence by the later post-exit years.

In-region marginal employment shows a consistent pattern where urban/town/rural mix graduates have the lowest rates (15.6%, 10.6%, 8.7%, 7.7%, 8.1% across the five years) relative to entirely rural and town/rural mix graduates, whose rates are more similar to each other. This pattern suggests that urban/town/ rural mix graduates who are not in sustained employment are more likely to be in other disconnected employment or no MN record than in local marginal work.

Overall, the annual Dem_Desc findings reveal a variable that is meaningfully more informative than the aggregated trajectory analysis suggested. The three community types produce distinct and interpretable annual profiles: town/rural mix graduates most anchored to regional employment, urban/town/rural mix graduates increasingly oriented toward statewide employment with the lowest regional attachment, and entirely rural graduates at growing risk of complete Minnesota labor market absence. These patterns warrant reconsideration of Dem_Desc’s potential contribution in multivariate models of detailed annual workforce states, even if its contribution to aggregated trajectory outcomes remains limited.

  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5

χ2=176.333 · df=8 · Cramer's V=0.082 · p=0.000


edr

Economic development region shows a statistically significant and substantively meaningful association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V the strongest of any high school characteristics variable and growing over time (V=0.110 at year 1, peaking at V=0.121 at year 4 before settling at V=0.115 at year 5). These effect sizes are notably larger than those observed in the aggregated trajectory analysis (V=0.053), confirming that the annual detailed analysis captures EDR’s labor market geography signal more precisely than the four-category trajectory framework.

The sustained statewide employment gap is the most dramatic and consistently growing difference across EDRs, and it is the defining feature of this variable throughout the five- year window. EDR 6E graduates show statewide employment rates far above both other EDRs at every time point: 13.0% vs. 8.7% and 7.8% at year 1, 15.2% vs. 10.1% and 8.9% at year 2, 17.6% vs. 11.8% and 9.9% at year 3, 19.7% vs. 12.7% and 10.8% at year 4, and 21.6% vs. 13.6% and 11.7% at year 5. The gap between EDR 6E and EDR 8 grows from 5.2 percentage points at year 1 to nearly 10 points at year 5 — the largest and most consistently widening gap in the entire annual EDR analysis. EDR 6E’s statewide employment advantage reflects the more economically connected labor market of the Southwest Central region and its proximity to regional hubs that facilitate out-of-region employment transitions.

The no MN employment record gap tells the complementary and equally striking story. EDR 8 graduates show the highest no MN record rates of any EDR at every time point: 33.2% vs. 24.0% and 27.6% at year 1, 34.8% vs. 26.0% and 29.3% at year 2, 36.0% vs. 27.3% and 31.6% at year 3, 37.5% vs. 28.0% and 33.9% at year 4, and 39.3% vs. 29.3% and 35.1% at year 5. The gap between EDR 8 and EDR 6E reaches 10 percentage points by year 5, growing steadily throughout. EDR 6W occupies a consistent middle position on no MN record rates at every year.

Sustained regional employment shows a notably different pattern — convergence rather than divergence over time. At year 1, EDR 6E graduates show the lowest sustained regional employment (20.9%) while EDR 8 graduates show the highest (23.1%), a modest gap that essentially disappears by year 2 (24.4%, 25.2%, 25.0%) and remains similar through year 5 (25.3%, 26.1%, 27.6%). The slight and growing EDR 8 advantage in sustained regional employment by years 4 and 5 is consistent with EDR 8 graduates being more regionally anchored — those who remain in Minnesota and achieve sustained employment do so locally at slightly higher rates than EDR 6E graduates.

The other disconnected employment category reveals another distinctive EDR 6E pattern. EDR 6E graduates show substantially higher other disconnected employment at year 1 (25.0% vs. 18.1% and 15.4%) and maintain elevated rates through the later years (15.1% vs. 12.4% and 9.8% at year 5). This elevated fragmented multi-employer or out-of-region non-sustained employment in EDR 6E likely reflects greater exposure to diverse labor market options — including out- of-region work that has not yet reached sustained thresholds — consistent with the more economically complex environment of the Southwest Central region.

In-region marginal employment shows EDR 6E with the lowest rates at every time point (17.1%, 12.8%, 10.5%, 8.8%, 8.7%), declining steadily and substantially over five years, while EDR 6W and EDR 8 show higher and more slowly declining rates. This pattern suggests that EDR 6E graduates who are not yet in sustained employment are less likely to be working locally in below-threshold positions and more likely to be in the other disconnected or statewide employment pathways.

Overall, the annual EDR findings reveal the most geographically interpretable pattern of any variable in the high school characteristics group. EDR 6E graduates are consistently more likely to achieve sustained statewide employment and less likely to be absent from Minnesota records, while EDR 8 graduates show the reverse — higher regional attachment among those who do find sustained work but substantially greater MN record absence overall. The growing and large gaps across EDRs in both statewide employment and no MN record rates across the five years make

  • after.ed.3
  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5

χ2=347.374 · df=8 · Cramer's V=0.115 · p=0.000


avg.unemp.rate

Average county unemployment rate shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with F-statistics growing consistently over time (F=43.0 at year 1, F=33.7 at year 2, F=41.7 at year 3, F=45.2 at year 4, F=47.9 at year 5), indicating a strengthening relationship as graduates settle into more stable labor market positions.

The pattern across all five years is consistent and reveals a clear ordering of workforce states by county unemployment environment. Sustained statewide employment is associated with the highest county unemployment rates at every time point — significantly above sustained regional employment by 0.40 points at year 1, 0.28 points at year 2, 0.27 points at year 3, 0.28 points at year 4, and 0.32 points at year 5. The year 1 gap is the largest single-year contrast observed across the entire five-year window, establishing from the outset that graduates who achieve sustained employment outside the region disproportionately originate from higher-unemployment counties. This labor market push dynamic is present immediately upon labor market entry and remains robust throughout.

Other disconnected employment — encompassing out-of- region non-sustained work and fragmented multi-employer patterns — shows the second highest unemployment rate environment at every year, also significantly above sustained regional employment (0.33 points at year 1, 0.23 points at year 2, 0.21 points at year 3, 0.18 points at year 4, 0.16 points at year 5). The declining magnitude of this gap over time suggests that as careers stabilize, the push dynamic becomes more specifically associated with sustained statewide employment rather than fragmented out-of-region work.

In-region marginal employment shows a more nuanced trajectory. At year 1 it is statistically indistinguishable from sustained regional employment (0.09 points above, p=0.093), but by years 3, 4, and 5 it falls significantly below sustained regional employment (−0.14, −0.16, and −0.12 points respectively). This shift suggests that workers trapped in local below-threshold employment by the middle post-exit years increasingly come from lower- unemployment counties — communities where local work is available but insufficient to reach sustained thresholds, rather than communities where push dynamics are driving workers out of the region.

No MN employment record shows the most interesting trajectory of any category. At years 1 and 2 it is statistically indistinguishable from sustained regional employment (0.04 and −0.05 points, both non- significant), but by years 4 and 5 it falls significantly below sustained regional employment (−0.10 and −0.09 points, both significant). This growing separation suggests that by the later post- exit years, graduates absent from Minnesota records are increasingly coming from lower-unemployment counties — consistent with out-of-state departure from communities where local conditions are relatively favorable, rather than displacement driven by high local unemployment.

The most consistent and analytically important pairwise contrast across all five years is between sustained statewide employment and both in-region marginal employment and no MN employment record — both of which score significantly lower on county unemployment rates than statewide workers at years 2 through 5. This three-tier separation — statewide workers from higher-unemployment counties, sustained regional workers in the middle, and in-region marginal workers and MN-record-absent graduates increasingly from lower-unemployment counties — becomes cleaner and more statistically robust in each successive year, making county unemployment rate a meaningful geographic labor market predictor in the annual detailed analysis despite its modest contribution to aggregated trajectory outcomes.

  • after.ed.1
  • after.ed.2
  • after.ed.3
  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5

avg.wages.pct.state

Average county wages as a percent of the state average shows statistically significant associations with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with F-statistics growing modestly over time (F=21.9 at year 1, F=24.9 at year 2, F=24.4 at year 3, F=26.0 at year 4, F=27.3 at year 5). While statistically robust, the absolute magnitude of differences across workforce states remains extremely small throughout — all contrasts fall within 0.02 percentage points of the state average — which severely limits practical significance regardless of statistical reliability.

The single most consistent and durable finding across all five years is that sustained statewide employment is associated with significantly lower county wages relative to sustained regional employment at every time point (−0.014 points at year 1, −0.015 at year 2, −0.017 at year 3, −0.014 at year 4, −0.017 at year 5). This finding is highly consistent in both direction and magnitude across the full five-year window and complements the county unemployment rate findings: graduates who achieve sustained statewide employment come from counties that are not only higher in unemployment but also lower in wages relative to the state average, reinforcing the labor market push interpretation.

Other disconnected employment also shows significantly lower county wages than sustained regional employment at every year (−0.012, −0.009, −0.010, −0.009, −0.008 points across years 1 through 5), though the magnitude declines slightly over time. This places other disconnected workers alongside statewide workers in coming from relatively lower-wage county environments, consistent with push dynamics driving fragmented out-of-region work as well as sustained statewide employment.

In-region marginal employment is statistically indistinguishable from sustained regional employment at every time point (differences of 0.000 to 0.005 points, none significant), consistent with both groups drawing from similar county wage environments and reinforcing the interpretation that in-region marginal workers are locally embedded rather than geographically mobile.

No MN employment record shows a more nuanced pattern. At year 1 it is not significantly different from sustained regional employment (−0.004 points, p=0.185), but becomes significantly lower at years 3 and 4 (−0.005 and −0.003 points respectively), before returning to non-significance at year 5 (−0.002 points, p=0.692). This inconsistent pattern across years suggests no stable wage-based differentiation for the MN record-absent group, in contrast to the cleaner unemployment rate gradient observed for this category.

The most analytically interesting pairwise contrast that emerges consistently across all five years is between in-region marginal employment and other disconnected employment — with in-region marginal workers consistently coming from higher-wage counties than other disconnected workers (differences of 0.010 to 0.014 points, all significant). This distinction suggests that the type of disconnection is geographically structured: locally underutilized workers come from relatively higher-wage county environments while those in fragmented out-of-region work come from lower-wage environments, pointing again to local labor market conditions as a driver of geographic labor market orientation.

Overall, county wages as a percent of the state average produces statistically significant but practically negligible associations at every time point. Its most durable and interpretable finding — that sustained statewide workers consistently originate from lower-wage counties — parallels the unemployment rate findings and adds modest confirmatory evidence for the labor market push interpretation. As in the full population and trajectory analyses, the negligible absolute magnitude of wage differences across groups supports its exclusion from multivariate models in favor of the more substantively differentiated county unemployment rate variable.

  • after.ed.1
  • after.ed.2
  • after.ed.3
  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5
