High school academics

Here are the variables we are exploring.

  • High school academics
    • pseo.participant
    • took.ACT
    • ACTCompositeScore
    • ap.exam
    • MCA.M
    • MCA.R
    • MCA.S
    • cte.achievement
    • total.cte.courses.taken

Summary

The annual detailed analysis of high school accomplishment variables confirms and substantially enriches the findings from the aggregated trajectory analysis. Effect sizes are generally larger and more consistent in the annual framework than the aggregated trajectory framework suggested, and the two-pathway structure — academic orientation toward statewide employment and CTE engagement toward regional employment — is clearly and durably present at every post-exit year.

CTE Achievement (V=0.107 to 0.122, growing) is the strongest categorical predictor in the annual analysis and produces the largest sustained category-level differences of any accomplishment variable. Its regional employment gradient — CTE concentrators at 25.6% to 30.9%, no CTE graduates at 14.8% to 16.9% across years 1 through 5 — grows consistently over time. The no MN employment record gap is equally striking, reaching 47.6% for no CTE graduates by year 5 compared to 29.9% for concentrators. The growing Cramer’s V confirms that CTE achievement is not just the strongest predictor but the one whose explanatory power increases most consistently over the five-year window.

Total CTE Courses Taken produces the highest and most consistent F-statistics of any continuous variable (F=90 to 101 across all five years) and maintains near-universal pairwise significance at every time point. Sustained regional employment consistently shows the highest mean CTE course volume, no MN employment record the lowest, with a stable 1.6 to 1.8 course gap between these two poles at every year. The growing separation between sustained regional and statewide employment on CTE volume over time is particularly important — confirming that CTE engagement specifically predicts regional rather than out-of-region labor market attachment.

ACT Participation (V=0.117 to 0.123) is the strongest categorical academic orientation predictor in the annual analysis, with effect sizes comparable to or exceeding CTE achievement at individual time points. Its dominant annual finding is the growing statewide employment gap — from 4.7 percentage points at year 1 to 8.2 points by year 5 — alongside consistently lower no MN record rates that converge toward non-participants by year 5 as higher- achieving non-completers increasingly leave Minnesota.

AP Exam Participation (V=0.053 to 0.086, growing) is distinctive as the only accomplishment variable whose annual Cramer’s V grows consistently at every time point. By year 5 it produces the largest statewide employment gap of any categorical variable at that time point (10.8 percentage points between AP participants and non- participants) and the sharpest regional employment deficit (8.2 points). Despite a small AP participant group, the growing divergence makes AP participation an increasingly important predictor in later post-exit years.

ACT Composite Score produces the most analytically complex annual pattern — a counterintuitive finding in which sustained regional employment consistently shows the lowest mean scores and no MN employment record shows among the highest, converging with sustained statewide employment into a high-scoring upper tier by year 5. This pattern reflects geographic departure among higher- achieving non-completers rather than labor market failure, and the three-tier structure — regional and in-region marginal workers at the lower end, statewide workers and no MN record at the upper end — becomes increasingly clean and statistically robust across the five years.

MCA Science (V=0.056 to 0.073, growing) produces the steepest statewide employment gradient of any MCA subject at year 5 (11.6 percentage points between level 1 and level 4) and the sharpest regional employment deficit for level 4 graduates. Its voluntary and self- selected nature limits multivariate modeling utility but it provides strong confirmatory evidence of the academic achievement gradient.

MCA Reading (V=0.061 to 0.077, growing at year 5) and MCA Math (V=0.055 to 0.067, stable) both produce the same core pattern: higher proficiency monotonically associated with higher statewide employment and lower in-region marginal employment, with sustained regional employment peaking at middle proficiency levels and level 4 showing the lowest regional employment of any proficiency category. Both subjects produce a U-shaped no MN record pattern that becomes more pronounced over time, reflecting the dual dynamic of low achievers disengaging locally and high achievers departing geographically. MCA Reading’s growing Cramer’s V at year 5 suggests it may become increasingly useful as an annual predictor, while MCA Math shows somewhat more stable associations across the five years.

PSEO Participation (V=0.062 to 0.083) shows a clearer and more nuanced annual pattern than the aggregated trajectory analysis suggested — particularly its initially lower no MN record rates that converge toward non-participants over time, alongside consistently higher statewide employment. Its effect sizes are modest but the annual framework reveals a genuine and interpretable academic pathway signature that the trajectory analysis understated.

MCA Science should be excluded from multivariate models due to its voluntary and self-selected nature. PSEO Participation and AP Exam Participation warrant careful consideration given their small group sizes and overlap with other academic orientation variables.

Three overarching conclusions emerge from the full annual accomplishments analysis. First, both CTE variables and academic orientation variables show their strongest and most interpretable associations in the annual detailed framework rather than the aggregated trajectory framework — validating the analytical investment of the annual approach. Second, the two-pathway structure is not just present but growing over time: the regional employment advantage for CTE-engaged graduates and the statewide employment advantage for academically oriented graduates both widen across the five post-exit years, indicating that early high school experiences have compounding rather than fading effects on labor market geography. Third, the no MN employment record category is the primary site where these two pathways diverge most dramatically — CTE graduates increasingly anchored in Minnesota, no-CTE graduates increasingly absent from it — making this category the analytically most important single workforce state for understanding the long-term consequences of high school program engagement among non-college completers.


pseo.participant

PSEO participation shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest and relatively stable across the five years (V=0.062 at year 1, peaking at V=0.083 at year 2 before declining to V=0.073 at year 5). As in the aggregated trajectory analysis, effect sizes are small and the variable’s most meaningful signal is concentrated in sustained statewide employment rather than regional employment or disconnection categories. Recall that this analysis is restricted to 2011 cohorts and later, reducing the sample to 8,951 individuals.

The sustained statewide employment gap is the most consistent and durable finding across all five years. PSEO participants show substantially higher statewide employment rates at every time point: 12.5% vs. 9.2% at year 1, 15.6% vs. 10.3% at year 2, 17.5% vs. 12.2% at year 3, 18.5% vs. 13.5% at year 4, and 20.8% vs. 14.6% at year 5. The gap grows from 3.3 percentage points at year 1 to 6.2 points by year 5, indicating that PSEO participants who did not complete a credential are increasingly oriented toward out-of-region statewide employment as careers develop — a pattern fully consistent with the academic pathway.

Sustained regional employment shows a modest and narrowing gap favoring non-PSEO graduates across all five years (22.9% vs. 22.4% at year 1, 25.5% vs. 23.8% at year 2, 26.6% vs. 24.7% at year 3, 26.5% vs. 25.3% at year 4, 26.7% vs. 24.3% at year 5), suggesting that PSEO participants are somewhat less likely to achieve sustained regional attachment — again consistent with academic orientation pulling toward statewide rather than regional employment.

No MN employment record shows the most interesting annual trajectory for PSEO participation. At year 1, PSEO participants show a notably lower no MN record rate than non-participants (23.2% vs. 29.2%) — a gap of 6 percentage points suggesting that PSEO participants are more engaged in the formal Minnesota labor market early on, despite their academic orientation. This gap narrows progressively over the five years (25.5% vs. 31.5% at year 2, 27.1% vs. 32.8% at year 3, 30.2% vs. 34.2% at year 4) before largely closing by year 5 (33.2% vs. 35.5%). The initial advantage in Minnesota labor market presence for PSEO participants diminishes over time as their statewide employment trajectory consolidates and some may move out of state.

Other disconnected employment is modestly elevated among PSEO participants at years 1 through 4 (21.6% vs. 19.5%, 20.2% vs. 16.5%, 18.4% vs. 14.6%, 16.4% vs. 13.1%) before converging at year 5 (13.3% vs. 11.9%). This elevated fragmented out-of-region employment in early years is consistent with PSEO participants having broader geographic labor market exposure that has not yet stabilized into sustained statewide employment. In-region marginal employment shows minimal differences across groups at most time points.

Overall, PSEO participation in the annual detailed analysis produces a clearer and more interpretable pattern than the aggregated trajectory analysis suggested. Its signature — lower no MN record rates early, higher statewide employment throughout, and growing geographic divergence from non-participants over time — is consistent with the academic pathway and provides a more nuanced picture than the trajectory analysis alone. However, effect sizes remain modest, and PSEO participation’s independent contribution in multivariate models alongside ACT participation and other academic orientation variables will require careful assessment.

  • after.ed.4
  • after.ed.5

took.ACT

ACT participation shows a statistically significant and meaningful association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V among the strongest of any accomplishment variable in the annual analysis and remaining relatively stable across the five years (V=0.117 at year 1, peaking at V=0.123 at year 3, then settling at V=0.115 at year 5). Unlike the aggregated trajectory analysis where ACT participation produced a strong but partly structural association, the annual detailed analysis reveals a substantively rich and consistent pattern that clarifies precisely how academic orientation shapes labor market geography among non-college completers.

The sustained statewide employment gap is the most dramatic and consistently growing difference across the five years. ACT takers show substantially higher statewide employment rates at every time point: 12.6% vs. 7.9% at year 1, 15.1% vs. 8.8% at year 2, 17.1% vs. 10.1% at year 3, 18.6% vs. 11.4% at year 4, and 20.4% vs. 12.2% at year 5. The gap grows from 4.7 percentage points at year 1 to 8.2 points by year 5 — a widening that represents the clearest and most durable annual signal for this variable. By year 5, ACT takers are more than one and a half times as likely as non-ACT graduates to be in sustained statewide employment, a substantial and growing geographic labor market advantage.

No MN employment record shows the second most consistent and consequential difference across years. Non-ACT graduates show substantially higher no MN record rates at every time point: 32.2% vs. 23.9% at year 1, 33.6% vs. 26.3% at year 2, 34.6% vs. 28.3% at year 3, 35.4% vs. 30.5% at year 4, and 36.7% vs. 32.2% at year 5. The gap starts at 8.3 percentage points at year 1 and narrows somewhat by year 5 (4.5 points), as ACT takers’ growing no MN record rate catches up — consistent with higher- achieving non-completers increasingly leaving Minnesota over time as identified in previous analyses. The year 1 gap is the largest of the five years and represents the most distinctive early post-exit difference between the two groups.

Sustained regional employment shows a modest and narrowing gap favoring non-ACT graduates across all five years (21.9% vs. 22.8% at year 1, growing to 27.3% vs. 25.4% at year 5). Non-ACT graduates are consistently somewhat more likely to achieve sustained regional attachment, a pattern that mirrors the academic pathway finding that CTE- oriented graduates without academic credentials are more regionally anchored than academically oriented non-completers.

Other disconnected employment is notably elevated among ACT takers at years 1 through 3 (21.8% vs. 17.6%, 18.8% vs. 15.9%, 17.3% vs. 14.2%) before converging by years 4 and 5 (14.5% vs. 13.0%, 12.5% vs. 12.0%). This elevated fragmented out- of-region employment in early years is consistent with ACT takers having broader geographic labor market exposure and orientation that has not yet stabilized into sustained statewide employment.

In-region marginal employment is modestly lower among ACT takers at years 1 through 4 (18.9% vs. 20.3%, 14.7% vs. 17.1%, 11.9% vs. 14.7%, 10.7% vs. 13.2%) before converging at year 5 (9.5% vs. 11.7%), confirming that ACT takers are less likely to be locally underutilized in below-threshold regional employment — their non-attachment tends toward geographic departure rather than local marginal work.

The annual ACT participation findings paint a coherent and geographically specific picture: ACT takers who did not complete a credential are systematically oriented toward out-of-region employment from the first post-exit year, with growing statewide employment, lower in-region marginal employment, and initially lower but converging no MN record rates relative to non- ACT graduates. The widening statewide employment gap over five years is the most important and durable finding, confirming that academic orientation shapes not just whether non-completers find work but specifically where they find it — a distinction with significant implications for regional labor market retention.

ACTCompositeScore

ACT Composite Score shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year among the subset of ACT takers, with F-statistics growing over time (F=18.6 at year 1, F=26.1 at year 2, F=23.1 at year 3, F=21.2 at year 4, F=29.5 at year 5). The pattern is analytically complex and requires careful interpretation — as in the aggregated trajectory analysis, the gradient does not run in the expected direction, with higher ACT scores associated with workforce states other than sustained regional employment.

At year 1, sustained regional employment shows the lowest mean ACT scores of any workforce state. All other categories score significantly above sustained regional workers: sustained statewide employment (+1.28 points, p=0.000), in-region marginal employment (+0.93 points, p=0.000), other disconnected employment (+0.91 points, p=0.000), and no MN employment record (+1.39 points, p=0.000). Strikingly, the no MN employment record group shows the highest mean scores at year 1, significantly above sustained regional employment by 1.39 points and not significantly different from sustained statewide employment (0.10 points, p=0.986). In-region marginal employment and other disconnected employment are statistically indistinguishable from each other and from sustained statewide employment. This early pattern establishes that among ACT takers, those who immediately enter sustained regional employment are the lowest scorers while those absent from Minnesota records are among the highest.

By year 2, the structure sharpens. Sustained statewide employment (−0.72 points below in-region marginal, significant) and no MN employment record separate from in-region marginal employment as distinct higher- scoring groups, while other disconnected employment moves upward to align with sustained statewide and no MN record (statistically indistinguishable from both). The four-category ordering at year 2 places sustained regional employment lowest, in-region marginal workers modestly above, and sustained statewide, other disconnected, and no MN record as a higher-scoring cluster — all significantly above regional workers by approximately 1.3 to 1.5 points.

At year 3, in-region marginal employment separates downward from sustained statewide employment and no MN employment record (−0.87 points below statewide, −1.17 points below no MN record, both significant), aligning more closely with sustained regional workers. Other disconnected employment remains elevated alongside sustained statewide and no MN record. By this point the structure is a clearer three-tier pattern: sustained regional and in-region marginal workers at the lower end, and sustained statewide, other disconnected, and no MN record at the upper end.

At year 4, in-region marginal employment has fully separated to align with sustained regional workers (−0.02 points, non-significant), while sustained statewide (+1.06 points), other disconnected (+0.98 points), and no MN record (+1.20 points) all remain significantly above regional workers. The three-tier structure is now well established.

At year 5, the pattern is most pronounced and cleanly structured. Sustained regional employment and in- region marginal employment are statistically indistinguishable (−0.24 points, p=0.830), forming a clear lower tier. Sustained statewide employment (+1.37 points above regional) and no MN employment record (+1.29 points above regional) form a high- scoring upper tier, statistically indistinguishable from each other (−0.08 points, p=0.986). Other disconnected employment falls in between but is significantly above regional workers (+0.67 points) and significantly below sustained statewide and no MN record workers at year 5.

The ACT Composite Score annual findings reinforce and extend the trajectory analysis interpretation. Higher-scoring ACT takers who did not complete a credential are not found in sustained regional employment — they are found in sustained statewide employment or absent from Minnesota employment records entirely, with these two groups converging to near-identical score profiles by year 5. The most analytically important implication is that no MN employment record among ACT takers does not represent low achievement or labor market failure — it represents high achievement with geographic departure, a pattern that becomes increasingly clear and statistically robust across the five post-exit years.

  • after.ed.5
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ after.ed.1, data = data.analysis)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = ACTCompositeScore ~ after.ed.1, data = data.analysis)

Terms:
                after.ed.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares     1417.96 104367.74
Deg. of Freedom          4      5481

Residual standard error: 4.363684
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
7691 observations deleted due to missingness
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

    95% family-wise confidence level

tukey_df <- TukeyHSD(summary) |> broom::tidy()
wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
addWorksheet(wb, "Tukey HSD")
writeData(wb, "Tukey HSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_college_deeper/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

tukey_df <- TukeyHSD(summary) |> broom::tidy()
wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
addWorksheet(wb, "Tukey HSD")
writeData(wb, "Tukey HSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_college_deeper/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore-3.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

ap.exam

AP exam participation shows a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest but growing over time (V=0.053 at year 1, V=0.060 at year 2, V=0.064 at year 3, V=0.073 at year 4, V=0.086 at year 5). This growing effect size — the opposite pattern from what was observed in the aggregated trajectory analysis — is analytically notable and reflects the increasing geographic divergence of AP participants over the five-year window. AP exam takers represent a small share of this population (869 of 13,177), which warrants caution in interpreting percentage differences given the smaller cell sizes.

The sustained statewide employment gap is the most consistent and growing difference across the five years. AP participants show substantially higher statewide employment rates at every time point: 14.7% vs. 9.5% at year 1, 17.4% vs. 11.0% at year 2, 18.5% vs. 12.6% at year 3, 21.6% vs. 13.9% at year 4, and 25.7% vs. 14.9% at year 5. The gap grows from 5.2 percentage points at year 1 to 10.8 points by year 5 — a near-doubling of the initial advantage that makes sustained statewide employment the most analytically important and durable annual finding for this variable. By year 5, AP participants are nearly twice as likely as non-participants to be in sustained statewide employment, the largest proportional gap of any accomplishment variable examined in the annual analysis for this category.

Sustained regional employment shows the complementary pattern — consistently lower among AP participants at every time point and the gap widening over time: 18.9% vs. 22.5% at year 1, 20.0% vs. 25.2% at year 2, 20.4% vs. 26.4% at year 3, 18.0% vs. 27.0% at year 4, and 18.8% vs. 27.0% at year 5. The gap grows from 3.6 points at year 1 to 8.2 points by year 5, indicating that AP participants are increasingly unlikely to be in sustained regional employment relative to non-participants as careers develop — the clearest annual regional attachment deficit of any academic orientation variable.

No MN employment record shows a more complex trajectory. At years 1 and 2, AP participants show modestly lower no MN record rates than non- participants (26.0% vs. 29.0%, 29.9% vs. 30.6%), suggesting somewhat greater early Minnesota labor market presence. By year 3, the rates are identical (32.0% vs. 32.0%), and by years 4 and 5 AP participants show marginally higher no MN record rates (34.5% vs. 33.3%, 35.8% vs. 34.8%). This convergence and slight reversal mirrors the ACT participation pattern and is consistent with higher- achieving non-completers gradually leaving Minnesota over the five-year window.

In-region marginal employment is consistently lower among AP participants across years 1 through 5 (17.6%, 13.0%, 9.6%, 9.4%, 6.6% for AP participants vs. 19.9%, 16.3%, 13.8%, 12.3%, 11.1% for non- participants), with the gap growing substantially over time. By year 5, AP participants are nearly half as likely as non-participants to be in in-region marginal employment, indicating that whatever local labor market engagement AP participants have is increasingly channeled toward sustained rather than marginal employment — or toward out-of-region statewide employment entirely.

Other disconnected employment is elevated among AP participants in early years (22.8% vs. 19.1% at year 1, 19.7% vs. 16.9% at year 2, 19.6% vs. 15.2% at year 3) before converging at years 4 and 5 (16.5% vs. 13.4%, 13.2% vs. 12.2%). This pattern of elevated fragmented out-of-region employment early in the post-exit window is consistent with the broader academic orientation pattern seen for ACT participation and PSEO participation.

Overall, AP exam participation produces the most dramatic and consistently growing statewide employment advantage of any categorical accomplishment variable in the annual detailed analysis, with its effect size growing every year rather than declining or stabilizing. Its signature — growing statewide employment, shrinking regional employment and in- region marginal employment, with converging no MN record rates — is fully consistent with the academic pathway and provides the clearest annual evidence of geographic labor market divergence driven by academic orientation among non-college completers.

MCA.M

MCA Math proficiency levels show a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest and relatively stable across the five years (V=0.056 at year 1, V=0.063 at year 2, V=0.059 at year 3, V=0.055 at year 4, V=0.067 at year 5). The four proficiency levels — from level 1 (below standard) through level 4 (exceeds standard) — produce a consistent and interpretable gradient across workforce states, though effect sizes are small throughout.

The sustained statewide employment gradient is the clearest and most consistent finding across all five years. Higher MCA Math proficiency is monotonically associated with higher statewide employment rates at every time point. At year 1: 8.2% (level 1), 9.9% (level 2), 12.0% (level 3), 13.4% (level 4). By year 5 the gradient steepens: 13.0% (level 1), 14.9% (level 2), 19.0% (level 3), 22.2% (level 4). The gap between level 1 and level 4 grows from 5.2 percentage points at year 1 to 9.2 points at year 5, a widening that mirrors the pattern observed for ACT participation and AP exam participation and confirms that academic achievement, regardless of how it is measured, is consistently associated with out-of-region labor market mobility.

In-region marginal employment shows the inverse gradient — higher proficiency monotonically associated with lower in-region marginal employment at years 1 through 5. At year 1: 23.9% (level 1), 20.4% (level 2), 18.9% (level 3), 19.0% (level 4). By year 5: 12.9% (level 1), 12.1% (level 2), 10.9% (level 3), 7.3% (level 4). The level 1 to level 4 gap grows from roughly 5 points at year 1 to nearly 6 points at year 5, with level 4 graduates showing by far the lowest in-region marginal employment by year 5. This gradient confirms that lower MCA Math proficiency is associated with local labor market underutilization, while higher proficiency is associated with either statewide employment or absence from Minnesota records.

Sustained regional employment shows a more complex non-monotonic pattern across proficiency levels at most time points. Levels 2 and 3 consistently show the highest regional employment rates — for example, at year 1: 21.9% (level 1), 26.2% (level 2), 25.2% (level 3), 22.0% (level 4). Level 1 and level 4 are both lower than the middle levels, suggesting that the lowest-achieving graduates are somewhat less likely to achieve sustained regional employment than middle-achieving graduates, while the highest- achieving graduates are pulled toward statewide employment. By year 5 the pattern persists: 28.5% (level 1), 31.5% (level 2), 28.4% (level 3), 24.6% (level 4), with level 4 graduates showing the lowest regional employment rates — the clearest signal that high math achievement routes non-completers away from regional attachment.

No MN employment record shows a U-shaped pattern at most time points, with level 1 and level 4 graduates showing higher no MN record rates than levels 2 and 3. At year 1: 27.0% (level 1), 22.2% (level 2), 22.3% (level 3), 24.6% (level 4). By year 5: 31.5% (level 1), 28.7% (level 2), 28.8% (level 3), 34.2% (level 4). The elevated no MN record rate among level 4 graduates at year 5 — 5.5 points above levels 2 and 3 — is consistent with high-achieving non-completers leaving Minnesota, mirroring the ACT Composite Score finding.

Other disconnected employment shows minimal and inconsistent variation across proficiency levels at most time points, suggesting this category is not strongly differentiated by MCA Math achievement.

Overall, MCA Math proficiency levels produce a coherent and consistent pattern in the annual detailed analysis: higher proficiency is associated with growing statewide employment and declining in-region marginal employment over time, while sustained regional employment peaks in the middle proficiency levels and declines among the highest- achieving graduates. The U-shaped no MN record pattern — with both lowest and highest achievers more likely to be absent from Minnesota records by year 5, albeit for different reasons — adds important nuance to the overall MCA Math gradient and reinforces the interpretation that low achievement predicts local disengagement while high achievement predicts geographic departure.

χ2=160.122 · df=12 · Cramer's V=0.067 · p=0.000


MCA.R

MCA Reading proficiency levels show a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest but growing over time (V=0.061 at year 1, V=0.065 at year 2, V=0.061 at year 3, V=0.068 at year 4, V=0.077 at year 5). The growing effect size at year 5 is noteworthy and distinguishes MCA Reading from MCA Math, which showed a more stable pattern. As with MCA Math, the four proficiency levels produce a consistent and interpretable pattern, though with some important differences in the character of the gradient.

The sustained statewide employment gradient is the clearest and most monotonic finding across all five years. Higher MCA Reading proficiency is consistently associated with higher statewide employment at every time point. At year 1: 7.2% (level 1), 8.6% (level 2), 11.6% (level 3), 12.5% (level 4). By year 5 the gradient steepens considerably: 10.6% (level 1), 14.2% (level 2), 17.8% (level 3), 20.4% (level 4). The gap between level 1 and level 4 grows from 5.3 percentage points at year 1 to 9.8 points at year 5, with the gradient becoming more pronounced at every successive year. This is the most consistent and durable finding for MCA Reading and mirrors the MCA Math pattern.

In-region marginal employment shows a clear monotonic inverse gradient — higher reading proficiency consistently associated with lower in-region marginal employment at every year. At year 1: 24.0% (level 1), 22.9% (level 2), 19.4% (level 3), 19.2% (level 4). By year 5: 15.3% (level 1), 11.3% (level 2), 12.0% (level 3), 8.5% (level 4). The level 1 to level 4 gap grows from roughly 5 points at year 1 to nearly 7 points at year 5, confirming that lower reading achievement is consistently associated with local labor market underutilization.

Sustained regional employment shows the same non- monotonic pattern observed for MCA Math — levels 2 and 3 consistently showing the highest regional employment rates, with both level 1 and level 4 lower. At year 1: 21.7% (level 1), 26.3% (level 2), 25.6% (level 3), 21.7% (level 4). At year 5: 27.6% (level 1), 33.0% (level 2), 30.5% (level 3), 24.3% (level 4). By year 5 level 4 graduates show the lowest sustained regional employment of any proficiency level — 9 percentage points below level 2 — the clearest signal across all five years that the highest reading achievers among non-completers are consistently routed away from regional employment.

No MN employment record again shows the U-shaped pattern familiar from MCA Math, but it is more pronounced for reading and develops more quickly. At year 1: 30.1% (level 1), 21.8% (level 2), 22.3% (level 3), 24.7% (level 4) — with level 1 already substantially elevated. By year 5 the U-shape deepens: 32.7% (level 1), 28.3% (level 2), 27.3% (level 3), 34.1% (level 4). The elevation of level 4 no MN record rates at year 5 — 6.8 points above levels 2 and 3 — is among the most striking single- cell differences in the MCA Reading data and is consistent with the interpretation that highest- achieving non-completers are increasingly likely to be absent from Minnesota records through out- of-state departure by the later post-exit years.

Other disconnected employment shows a modest positive gradient — higher reading proficiency slightly associated with higher other disconnected employment at most time points — a pattern consistent with higher-achieving graduates having more exposure to fragmented out-of-region work that has not yet stabilized into sustained statewide employment.

The MCA Reading pattern across five years closely parallels MCA Math but with two notable differences. First, the growing Cramer’s V at year 5 (V=0.077 vs. V=0.067 for MCA Math) suggests that reading proficiency is becoming a stronger annual predictor over time rather than remaining stable, which may reflect reading skills’ increasing relevance for accessing higher-quality out-of-region employment. Second, the U-shaped no MN record pattern is more pronounced and develops earlier for reading than for math, with level 1 graduates already showing substantially elevated no MN record rates at year 1. Together, these two MCA measures tell a substantively consistent story that reinforces the conclusion from the continuous ANOVA analyses: academic achievement shapes labor market geography among non-college completers, with higher achievers systematically oriented toward statewide employment or out-of-state departure and lower achievers toward local labor market underutilization or disengagement.

χ2=208.498 · df=12 · Cramer's V=0.077 · p=0.000


MCA.S

MCA Science proficiency levels show a statistically significant association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V modest and relatively stable across the five years (V=0.056 at year 1, V=0.060 at year 2, V=0.053 at year 3, V=0.063 at year 4, V=0.073 at year 5). The growing effect size at year 5 mirrors the MCA Reading pattern and distinguishes both from MCA Math, which remained more stable. As noted throughout the analysis, MCA Science is voluntary and self-selected, and level 4 represents a small group (n=529 at each year) concentrated among the most academically oriented students — factors that shape interpretation throughout. The reduced sample of 9,151 versus 11,873 for MCA Math and 11,702 for MCA Reading also reflects this selectivity.

The sustained statewide employment gradient is the most consistent finding and replicates the MCA Math and Reading patterns with high fidelity. Higher science proficiency is monotonically associated with higher statewide employment at every time point. At year 1: 7.9% (level 1), 10.8% (level 2), 12.5% (level 3), 12.5% (level 4). By year 5 the gradient is dramatically steeper: 12.4% (level 1), 16.1% (level 2), 20.0% (level 3), 24.0% (level 4). The gap between level 1 and level 4 grows from 4.6 percentage points at year 1 to 11.6 points at year 5 — the largest proficiency gradient of any MCA subject at year 5, a finding that likely reflects the strong overlap between science achievement and academic orientation toward STEM fields that route graduates into out-of-region employment.

Sustained regional employment shows the now-familiar non-monotonic pattern, but with a distinctive feature unique to MCA Science: level 4 graduates show the sharpest and most consistent regional employment deficit of any proficiency level across all five years. At year 1: 24.8% (level 1), 26.4% (level 2), 23.4% (level 3), 18.0% (level 4). By year 5: 30.0% (level 1), 31.2% (level 2), 26.9% (level 3), 22.1% (level 4). The level 4 deficit relative to levels 1 and 2 grows substantially over the five years — reaching nearly 9 percentage points below level 2 at year 5 — and is larger than the equivalent level 4 deficit for MCA Math or Reading. This further reinforces the interpretation that the highest science achievers among non-completers are the most strongly oriented away from regional employment.

In-region marginal employment shows a clear monotonic inverse gradient at years 1 through 5, with higher proficiency consistently associated with lower marginal employment. At year 1: 24.6% (level 1), 19.9% (level 2), 19.0% (level 3), 22.1% (level 4) — with level 4 slightly elevated, possibly reflecting small cell instability. By year 5 the gradient is cleaner: 14.7% (level 1), 11.7% (level 2), 9.0% (level 3), 5.9% (level 4) — a spread of nearly 9 percentage points and the sharpest in-region marginal employment gradient of any MCA subject at year 5.

No MN employment record shows the U-shaped pattern familiar from MCA Math and Reading but with a notable difference — the U-shape for MCA Science is less pronounced and develops more slowly than for the other two subjects. At year 1, all four levels show relatively similar no MN record rates (23.4%, 20.6%, 24.7%, 24.8%) with no clear gradient. By year 5, however, level 4 emerges as clearly elevated (35.7% vs. 29.6%, 28.0%, 31.2% for levels 1, 2, and 3 respectively), consistent with out-of-state departure among the highest science achievers — but the gap is smaller and later-developing than the equivalent pattern for MCA Reading.

Other disconnected employment shows no meaningful gradient across proficiency levels at any time point, with all four levels producing rates within one to two percentage points of each other consistently.

The MCA Science annual findings replicate the core pattern observed for MCA Math and Reading — higher proficiency associated with statewide employment and lower regional employment — but with some important differences. The statewide employment gradient at year 5 is the steepest of any MCA subject, and the regional employment deficit for level 4 graduates is the most pronounced, suggesting that high science achievement is the most powerful academic achievement signal of geographic labor market departure among non-college completers. The voluntary and self-selected nature of MCA Science limits its use in multivariate models, but its confirmatory role in the annual analysis is clear and consistent with the broader academic achievement pattern identified throughout.

χ2=144.901 · df=12 · Cramer's V=0.073 · p=0.000


cte.achievement

CTE achievement shows a statistically significant and growing association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V among the strongest of any variable in the annual accomplishments analysis and increasing consistently over time (V=0.107 at year 1, V=0.110 at year 2, V=0.109 at year 3, V=0.122 at year 4, V=0.117 at year 5). This growing effect size — the strongest sustained pattern of any accomplishment variable across all five years — confirms CTE achievement as the most powerful annual predictor of detailed workforce participation states in this population.

The sustained regional employment gradient is the defining and most dramatic feature of the CTE achievement annual data. The gap between CTE concentrators and the no CTE group is present from year 1 and grows substantially over time. At year 1: 25.6% (concentrators), 22.4% (participants), 14.8% (no CTE). By year 5: 30.9% (concentrators), 26.3% (participants), 16.9% (no CTE). The gap between CTE concentrators and the no CTE group grows from 10.8 percentage points at year 1 to 14.0 points at year 5, and the gradient across all three CTE categories is monotonic and consistent at every time point. By year 5, CTE concentrators are nearly twice as likely as the no CTE group to be in sustained regional employment — the largest category-level difference observed for any variable in the entire annual accomplishments analysis.

The no MN employment record gap is equally striking and runs in the opposite direction. No CTE graduates show dramatically higher no MN record rates than CTE concentrators at every time point, with the gap large and growing: 40.0% vs. 25.0% at year 1 (15-point gap), 42.5% vs. 26.0% at year 2 (16.5 points), 43.9% vs. 27.6% at year 3 (16.3 points), 46.5% vs. 28.2% at year 4 (18.3 points), and 47.6% vs. 29.9% at year 5 (17.7 points). By year 5, nearly half of all no CTE graduates carry no Minnesota employment record — the single most alarming annual finding in the entire accomplishments analysis. CTE participants occupy a consistent middle position (26.4% to 33.4% across years 1 through 5), showing a clear dose-response pattern in which more CTE engagement is associated with lower no MN record rates at every time point.

Sustained statewide employment shows modest and relatively similar rates across CTE categories at most time points, with concentrators and participants showing slightly higher rates than the no CTE group but without the dramatic gradient observed for regional employment and no MN record. At year 5: 16.0% (concentrators), 16.3% (participants), 13.9% (no CTE). This near-convergence of statewide employment across CTE categories confirms that CTE achievement is specifically and powerfully predictive of regional rather than statewide labor market attachment — it does not route graduates away from or toward out-of-region employment but rather anchors them locally.

In-region marginal employment shows a consistent but modest gradient, with CTE concentrators and participants showing somewhat higher rates than no CTE graduates across most years. At year 1: 20.0% (concentrators), 20.5% (participants), 18.1% (no CTE). By year 5: 11.5% (concentrators), 11.0% (participants), 9.0% (no CTE). The declining rates for all groups over time reflect the expected conversion from marginal to sustained employment, but the slight elevation for CTE groups suggests they remain more locally engaged even in below- threshold positions.

Other disconnected employment shows minimal variation across CTE categories at most time points, with all three groups producing rates within a few percentage points of each other.

The annual CTE achievement findings tell the most powerful and consistent story of any accomplishment variable in the detailed analysis. CTE engagement — particularly at the concentrator level — is the single strongest annual predictor of sustained regional employment and the single strongest annual predictor of reduced no MN employment record rates. These two effects operate together and grow over time, producing an increasingly pronounced divide between CTE-engaged graduates who anchor themselves to the regional economy and no-CTE graduates who are increasingly absent from Minnesota employment entirely. The effect size at year 4 (V=0.122) is the highest annual Cramer’s V observed for any variable in the entire accomplishments analysis, making CTE achievement the dominant predictor of detailed annual workforce participation states among non-college completers in Southwest Minnesota.

χ2=357.996 · df=8 · Cramer's V=0.117 · p=0.000


total.cte.courses.taken

Total CTE courses taken shows a statistically significant and exceptionally robust association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with F-statistics among the highest of any continuous variable in the entire analysis and remaining consistently large across all five years (F=94.7 at year 1, F=98.2 at year 2, F=90.4 at year 3, F=101.3 at year 4, F=92.4 at year 5). As in the aggregated trajectory analysis, nearly every pairwise Tukey contrast is statistically significant, making this the most robustly differentiated continuous variable in the annual detailed framework.

The dominant and consistent finding across all five years is that sustained regional employment is associated with the highest mean CTE course-taking of any workforce state, and no MN employment record is associated with the lowest — with all other categories falling between these two poles. This ordering is statistically significant and remarkably stable across the five post-exit years.

Sustained regional employment shows the highest mean CTE courses at every time point. No MN employment record falls significantly below at all five years, with the gap ranging from 1.77 courses below regional at year 1 to 1.73 courses below at year 2, 1.61 at year 3, 1.71 at year 4, and 1.63 at year 5. This gap — consistently between 1.6 and 1.8 courses — is the largest and most stable pairwise contrast across the entire five-year window and confirms that complete absence from Minnesota employment is systematically associated with the lowest CTE course-taking of any annual workforce state.

Sustained statewide employment falls significantly below sustained regional employment at all five years (−0.44 courses at year 1, −0.60 at year 2, −0.52 at year 3, −0.81 at year 4, −0.73 at year 5), with the gap growing substantially from year 1 to years 4 and 5. This growing separation between regional and statewide employment on CTE course- taking is one of the most analytically important annual findings: as careers stabilize, the CTE course volume advantage for regional workers relative to statewide workers becomes increasingly pronounced, consistent with CTE engagement being specifically and durably predictive of regional rather than out-of-region labor market attachment.

In-region marginal employment falls significantly below sustained regional employment at every year (−0.76 at year 1, −0.59 at year 2, −0.51 at year 3, −0.62 at year 4, −0.65 at year 5), while other disconnected employment falls even further below (−0.87 at year 1, −0.93 at year 2, −0.95 at year 3, −1.11 at year 4, −0.98 at year 5). Notably, in-region marginal and sustained statewide employment are statistically indistinguishable from each other at most time points — both falling in a middle tier between sustained regional employment at the top and no MN employment record at the bottom.

The pairwise structure at each year can be summarized as a consistent three-tier ordering: sustained regional employment at the highest CTE volume, a middle cluster of sustained statewide, in-region marginal, and other disconnected employment (largely indistinguishable from each other), and no MN employment record at the lowest volume. This three-tier pattern is present at every time point and grows more statistically distinct over the five years as the no MN record and other disconnected contrasts with sustained regional employment remain large and all significant.

The annual total CTE courses findings confirm and extend the aggregated trajectory analysis: CTE course volume is the strongest and most consistent continuous predictor of sustained regional employment in this population, operating through a mechanism of regional labor market attachment that becomes more entrenched — not less — as careers develop over the five post-exit years. The consistently large F-statistics and near- universal pairwise significance across all years make total CTE courses taken the most analytically powerful continuous variable in the entire annual detailed workforce participation analysis.

summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ after.ed.1, data = data.analysis)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = total.cte.courses.taken ~ after.ed.1, data = data.analysis)

Terms:
                after.ed.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares     5547.22 192924.74
Deg. of Freedom          4     13172

Residual standard error: 3.827085
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = total.cte.courses.taken ~ after.ed.1, data = data.analysis)

tukey_df <- TukeyHSD(summary) |> broom::tidy()
wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
addWorksheet(wb, "Tukey HSD")
writeData(wb, "Tukey HSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_college_deeper/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

Post-secondary

Summary

Postsecondary attendance shows a statistically significant and substantively meaningful association with detailed annual workforce participation states at every post-exit year, with Cramer’s V the highest of any variable at year 1 (V=0.178) and declining but remaining meaningful across subsequent years (V=0.154 at year 2, V=0.123 at year 3, V=0.119 at year 4, V=0.101 at year 5). The declining effect size over time reflects the gradual convergence of the two groups as early differences in statewide employment and no MN record rates narrow — but meaningful differences persist throughout all five years.

The sustained statewide employment gap is the most dramatic and consistent finding across all five years. Postsecondary attendees show substantially higher statewide employment at every time point: 15.5% vs. 6.5% at year 1 — a gap of nine percentage points that is the largest single annual difference in the entire attended.ps analysis. The gap narrows but remains large across subsequent years: 16.7% vs. 8.3% at year 2 (8.4 points), 17.7% vs. 10.2% at year 3 (7.5 points), 19.4% vs. 11.4% at year 4 (8.0 points), and 20.0% vs. 13.0% at year 5 (7.0 points). The consistency of this gap — holding between 7 and 9 percentage points across all five years — makes sustained statewide employment the most durable annual signal for postsecondary attendance and confirms that the postsecondary experience, even without credential completion, is strongly associated with out-of-region labor market orientation throughout the post-exit window.

The no MN employment record gap shows the second most consistent and substantial difference, running in the opposite direction. Non-attendees show higher no MN record rates at every time point: 31.9% vs. 23.5% at year 1 (8.4-point gap), 33.4% vs. 25.8% at year 2 (7.6 points), 33.9% vs. 28.7% at year 3 (5.2 points), 35.0% vs. 30.6% at year 4 (4.4 points), and 36.4% vs. 32.4% at year 5 (4.0 points). The gap narrows steadily over the five years as postsecondary attendees’ no MN record rates grow toward non-attendees, consistent with higher- achieving non-completers increasingly leaving Minnesota over time — a pattern now familiar across multiple academic orientation variables.

Sustained regional employment shows a more complex trajectory. At year 1, postsecondary attendees show higher regional employment (25.2% vs. 20.5%) — a gap of 4.7 percentage points that reverses the naive expectation that never-attendees would be more regionally anchored. By years 3 through 5, the gap narrows considerably (27.5% vs. 25.1% at year 3, 27.2% vs. 26.0% at year 4, 26.9% vs. 26.2% at year 5), and regional employment rates converge to near-identical levels by year 5. This convergence indicates that postsecondary attendance does not persistently reduce regional employment attachment among those who remain in Minnesota — it primarily redirects workers toward statewide rather than regional employment.

In-region marginal employment shows the reverse pattern from regional employment — non-attendees consistently show higher in-region marginal rates, particularly in early years (22.0% vs. 15.9% at year 1, 17.8% vs. 13.3% at year 2), with the gap narrowing to modest levels by year 5 (11.8% vs. 9.2%). This indicates that never-attendees who are not yet in sustained employment are more likely to be working locally below threshold — a local underutilization pattern consistent with the finding that non-attendees tend toward regional but marginal labor market engagement rather than statewide employment.

Other disconnected employment shows minimal and largely non-significant differences across groups at every time point (19.1% vs. 19.8% at year 1, converging to 12.7% vs. 11.5% at year 5), suggesting this category is not meaningfully differentiated by postsecondary attendance.

The annual postsecondary attendance findings tell a coherent and important story. In the first year after exiting formal education, postsecondary attendees are substantially more likely to be in statewide employment and substantially less likely to be absent from Minnesota records or in in-region marginal employment — suggesting that the postsecondary experience itself provides early labor market advantages that route graduates toward more stable and geographically broader employment. Over time, statewide employment gaps persist while regional employment converges, painting a picture of postsecondary attendance as a geographic labor market orienter rather than a regional attachment factor — consistent with the academic pathway identified throughout the full analysis.

Summary: Annual Detailed Workforce Participation States

Analysis — Full Variable Group Overview

The annual analysis of detailed workforce participation states across the five post-exit years represents the most granular and analytically rich component of the non-college completer study. By disaggregating workforce outcomes into five categories — sustained regional employment, sustained statewide employment, in-region marginal employment, other disconnected employment, and no MN employment record — and examining each across five annual time points, this analysis reveals patterns that the aggregated trajectory framework obscured or understated. Several cross-cutting conclusions emerge.

The Annual Framework Adds Meaningful Analytical Value

Across virtually every variable group, effect sizes in the annual detailed analysis are larger and more interpretable than those in the aggregated trajectory analysis. Community demographic description, which produced a negligible V=0.025 in the trajectory analysis, shows V=0.073 to 0.087 annually. EDR shows V=0.110 to 0.121 annually versus V=0.053 in the trajectory analysis. Postsecondary attendance shows V=0.178 at year 1 versus V=0.132 in the trajectory. The annual framework does not simply replicate the trajectory findings at higher resolution — it reveals genuinely new information about which workforce states are being differentiated and how those differences evolve over time.

The No MN Employment Record Category Is the Primary Fault Line Across All Variable Groups

Across demographic, high school characteristics, high school accomplishments, and postsecondary variables, the no MN employment record category is consistently the largest site of differentiation. Every significant demographic disadvantage — being female, an English learner, non-English home language, American Indian, special education — is associated primarily with elevated no MN record rates rather than lower sustained regional employment per se. Every academic orientation indicator — ACT participation, AP exams, higher MCA scores, postsecondary attendance — is associated with lower no MN record rates early and growing no MN record rates later, as higher-achieving non-completers increasingly leave Minnesota. CTE engagement is associated with the lowest no MN record rates of any accomplishment category. The no MN employment record category is not a single phenomenon — it reflects out-of-state departure among higher achievers, informal employment or geographic departure among language minority graduates, and labor market disengagement among the most disconnected — but it is the most analytically important single annual workforce state in the entire analysis.

Two Distinct Disconnection Signatures Emerge

The annual analysis reveals that disconnection is not a uniform condition. Two distinct signatures recur across variable groups:

Local underutilization — characterized by elevated in-region marginal employment alongside lower no MN record rates — is associated with special education status, economic disadvantage, lower CTE engagement, and lower academic achievement. These graduates are present in the Minnesota labor market but unable to achieve sustained thresholds. Their disconnection is a regional labor market problem.

Geographic absence — characterized by elevated no MN record rates without corresponding elevation in in-region marginal employment — is associated with English learner status, non-English home language, American Indian background, female gender, and — paradoxically — higher academic achievement among non-completers. For the disadvantaged subgroups, absence likely reflects informal employment, out- of-state residence, or labor markets not captured by wage records. For higher achievers, it reflects out-of-state departure.

These two signatures require different policy responses and different multivariate modeling approaches, and their identification is one of the most important contributions of the annual detailed analysis.

Rankings by Variable Group

Among demographic variables, special education status (peak V=0.104) and gender (peak V=0.095) show the strongest and most consistent annual associations, followed by English learner status (peak V=0.082) and non-English home language (peak V=0.088). Race/ethnicity shows a modest overall Cramer’s V (V=0.050 to 0.055) but contains the most extreme subgroup finding in the analysis — American Indian graduates with 40% to 47% no MN record rates across years 1 through 5. Economic status shows the weakest and most counterintuitive annual pattern, with disadvantaged graduates actually showing more local labor market engagement than non-disadvantaged graduates in early years. Community demographic description, negligible in the trajectory analysis, shows meaningful annual associations (V=0.073 to 0.087) driven primarily by the urban/town/rural mix statewide employment advantage and the entirely rural no MN record growth trajectory.

Among high school characteristics variables, EDR is the strongest predictor (V=0.110 to 0.121), with EDR 6E’s growing statewide employment advantage and EDR 8’s growing no MN record disadvantage representing the clearest geographic labor market differentiation in the analysis. County unemployment rate produces interpretable push dynamic findings at every year, with statewide workers consistently from higher-unemployment counties. County wages adds confirmatory but practically negligible information.

Among high school accomplishments variables, CTE achievement (V=0.107 to 0.122) and ACT participation (V=0.117 to 0.123) are the two strongest categorical predictors, with AP exam participation (V=0.053 to 0.086, growing) emerging as increasingly important in later years. Total CTE courses taken produces the strongest and most consistent continuous variable findings (F=90 to 101), maintaining near-universal pairwise significance across all five years. ACT Composite Score produces the most analytically complex finding — a counterintuitive positive association between scores and persistent disconnection that reflects geographic departure rather than labor market failure. MCA Reading and Math produce consistent but smaller academic achievement gradients, while MCA Science provides confirmatory evidence despite its measurement limitations.

Postsecondary attendance (V=0.101 to 0.178) is the strongest single variable at year 1 in the annual analysis and remains among the strongest predictors across all five years, with its primary signature being a large and durable statewide employment advantage for attendees alongside lower early no MN record rates that converge over time.

The Two-Pathway Structure Is Confirmed and Growing

The most important overarching conclusion from the annual detailed analysis is that the two-pathway structure identified in the full population analysis is not only present among non-college completers but becomes more pronounced over time. The academic pathway — ACT participation, AP exams, higher MCA scores, postsecondary attendance — produces growing statewide employment advantages and growing regional employment deficits across the five post-exit years. The CTE pathway — CTE achievement, total CTE courses taken — produces growing regional employment advantages and growing no MN record deficits across the same five years. These two pathways diverge more rather than less as careers develop, suggesting that the labor market geography of non-college completers is largely determined by high school program engagement and does not substantially self-correct over the first five post-exit years.


The Story the Data Tell: Workforce Trajectories of Non-College Completers in Southwest Minnesota

Among Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who never earned a postsecondary credential, the first five years after exiting formal education tell a story of rapid and largely irreversible sorting — sorting that begins in high school, accelerates immediately upon labor market entry, and becomes more entrenched with every passing year.

The Sorting Happens Fast

Within the very first year after exiting formal education, the broad contours of where a non-college completer will end up are already visible. Those with deep CTE engagement show up in regional employment early and stay. Those with strong academic orientation show up in statewide employment or disappear from Minnesota records entirely. Those with neither credential pathway — no meaningful CTE engagement and no academic orientation — are already the most likely to be absent from formal Minnesota employment in year 1, and that absence grows every year thereafter.

This is not a story of gradual adjustment, of young people finding their footing over time. The sorting is essentially complete by year 2 or 3. After that, the gaps widen but the rank ordering of groups almost never changes.

The Modal Experience Is Disconnection

The single most important finding in the entire analysis is also the most sobering: among non- college completers, the most common experience during the first five years after exiting formal education is persistent disconnection. Half of all non-college completers — 50.4% — are disconnected in four or five of their first five post-exit years. When you add broadly disconnected graduates, nearly two-thirds spend most of their early adult years outside sustained employment. This is not a fringe population experiencing temporary turbulence. It is the majority experience of non-college completers in this region.

And it gets worse over time, not better. The no MN employment record category — the most severe form of labor market absence — grows from 28.8% in year 1 to 34.9% by year 5. At year 10 in the point-in-time analysis, 38.6% of non-college completers carry no Minnesota employment record at all, a decade after graduating high school.

Two Pathways, Two Very Different Destinations

Within the non-completer population, high school experiences create two recognizable and diverging trajectories.

The first belongs to graduates with strong CTE engagement. CTE concentrators show the highest sustained regional employment rates of any group at every time point — reaching nearly 31% by year 5 compared to just 17% for graduates with no CTE engagement. They show the lowest no MN employment record rates. Their disconnection, when it occurs, tends to be local and transitional rather than permanent and geographically absent. They stay. They work. They anchor themselves to the regional economy. And critically, the more CTE courses a graduate took — regardless of achievement level — the better their trajectory outcomes. The relationship is dose-dependent and remarkably consistent.

The second pathway belongs to graduates with academic orientation — those who took the ACT, participated in AP courses or PSEO, earned higher MCA scores. These graduates, even without completing a credential, are pulled toward statewide employment outside the region. Their statewide employment rates grow every year relative to their less academically oriented peers, reaching more than double the rate by year 5. And increasingly over time, the highest- achieving among them simply disappear from Minnesota employment records altogether — not because they failed to find work, but because they found it somewhere else. The region’s investment in developing their academic capacity left with them.

The Absent Group: Who They Are and Why It Matters

The no MN employment record category is the most analytically important and practically troubling finding in the analysis. By year 5, more than a third of all non-college completers carry no Minnesota employment record. By year 10, that share approaches 39%.

But this category is not monolithic. The annual analysis reveals at least two distinct populations within it.

Among higher-achieving graduates — those with ACT participation, AP exam experience, higher MCA scores, postsecondary attendance — absence from Minnesota records appears to reflect geographic departure. These graduates left. Whether they found work in other states, pursued opportunities unavailable in Southwest Minnesota, or simply followed the pull of broader labor markets, they are not present in the regional or statewide economy. Their absence is a talent drain.

Among disadvantaged subgroups — English learners, graduates from non-English speaking homes, American Indian graduates, women — absence from Minnesota records tells a different and more troubling story. These graduates show no MN record rates that are elevated from the very first post-exit year, with no corresponding elevation in statewide employment. They are not appearing to leave for better opportunities elsewhere. Something else is happening — informal employment, agricultural or seasonal work not captured in wage records, caregiving, or genuine labor market withdrawal. For American Indian graduates, 40% to 47% carry no MN record at every annual time point. For English learners, the rate grows from 36% at year 1 to 40% by year 5. These are populations for whom formal labor market engagement — of any kind, in any location — is persistently out of reach or simply not captured.

What High School Does — and Doesn’t — Determine

The most consistent finding across every analytical framework in this study is that what students did in high school matters more than who they are. Demographic characteristics — race, gender, economic status, special education status — are associated with trajectory outcomes, but these associations are smaller and less consistent than the associations produced by high school program engagement. CTE course volume, CTE achievement level, ACT participation, and postsecondary attendance all outperform demographic variables as predictors of where non-college completers end up.

This is both encouraging and sobering. Encouraging, because it suggests that program-level interventions — expanding CTE access and engagement, supporting postsecondary attendance even when completion is uncertain — have genuine leverage on outcomes. Sobering, because the students who most need that leverage are often those least connected to the programs that provide it. The no-CTE, no-academic- orientation group — the graduates who slip through both pathways — is already the highest-risk subpopulation at year 1 and remains so through year 5. They are not being captured by either the CTE pathway or the academic pathway, and the data provide no mechanism within high school that is currently reaching them.

The Regional Economy’s Dependence on a Narrow Pathway

Southwest Minnesota’s regional labor market depends heavily on workers who choose to stay. The analysis makes clear that only one high school pathway consistently produces those workers: deep CTE engagement. CTE concentrators and graduates with high CTE course volumes are the most reliably regionally attached group among non-college completers. They achieve sustained regional employment earlier, maintain it more consistently, and carry lower no MN record rates than any other subgroup.

But CTE engagement alone cannot sustain a regional economy. The academic pathway — which reliably develops talent — reliably exports it. Postsecondary attendance without completion leaves graduates in statewide employment outside the region. High ACT scores predict departure. The region is systematically developing human capital it then loses.

This tension between credential pathways and regional retention is not a new problem, but the data quantify it with unusual precision. The gap between what the academic pathway produces and what the region retains grows every year across the five post-exit window. By year 5, graduates with strong academic orientation are more than twice as likely to be in statewide employment outside the region as their less academically oriented peers, and increasingly likely to have left Minnesota entirely.

The Bottom Line

Non-college completers in Southwest Minnesota are not a homogeneous population experiencing uniform outcomes. They are a deeply divided population, sorted rapidly and durably by high school program experiences into trajectories that become more divergent — not less — with every passing year.

One group anchors to the region through CTE engagement. Another leaves through academic orientation. A third — the largest single group — drifts into persistent absence from formal employment, neither anchored nor departed but simply unreachable by the formal labor market systems that track outcomes and deliver interventions.

The regional workforce challenge is therefore not one problem but three: retaining those the academic pathway develops and exports, stabilizing those whom marginal regional employment has not yet converted to sustained attachment, and most urgently, reconnecting the large and growing share of non-college completers who have effectively disappeared from both the regional economy and Minnesota’s formal labor market entirely.
